Dear Ahmet, please know that we stand in solidarity with you, and that we support the democratic struggle your party is conducting in Turkey for the recognition of the fundamental rights of the Kurdish people.
We recognize in the DTP an indispensable instrument for promoting democracy in Turkey, and for this reason we firmly condemn the police operations being carried out by the Turkish government.
Prime Minister Erdogan has ordered action against you, imprisoning all three vice-presidents of the DTP, along with over 300 activists and supporters.
The DTP's victory in the most recent local elections demonstrates that the DTP is a party with strong popular democratic support.

The European Parliament, in its resolutions, has called on Prime Minister Erdogan to engage in direct talks with the DTP, yet his response has been increased repression, more police, greater authoritarianism, and more imprisonment.

Everyone knows that my parliamentary group and the Left in Europe have so far supported Turkey's accession process to the European Union, while simultaneously backing the reform process in that country.

I do believe, however, that in the past two years—especially in recent months—Erdogan has revealed another side, that of a leader compromised with the worst elements of the Turkish army, intent solely on massacring the Kurds.
The reform process is practically dead, prisons are filling up with Kurds. It is Erdogan himself who is closing off any prospect of Turkey's accession.
It is solely his responsibility, and only his, for what is happening in euro-Turkish relations and for the growing skepticism of European public opinion toward Turkey.

I want to send a very strong political message to Erdogan: either make the search for a negotiated political solution to the Kurdish issue a priority—clearly, through direct talks with the DTP—or shall it be us who demand the suspension of the accession negotiations with the European Union?

The path to Turkey's accession to the Union passes through Diyarbakır and the DTP; otherwise, it will be we—those who have so far supported the accession process—who will have to request a pause for reflection that risks being definitive.

The European Parliament undertakes its own efforts in order to comply with others.

This resolution calls on the Commission to close, by 2009, a legislative gap concerning the ban on second-hand products containing asbestos, including roof tiles, aircraft parts, and similar items, which should be permanently disposed of.
If they set specific dates, once again, for a strategy to ban all types of asbestos by 2015, these were goals already established in 1999.
Ten years have passed and killing has continued.
The GUE had claimed, among the first initiatives of this legislative term, the establishment of a community compensation fund for victims and dedicated funds for decontamination.
The request was specific toward the Commission, which today instead flattens itself behind the will of multinational corporations.
It is necessary instead to move on to concrete facts and commitments.
Only when these demands stem from the same requests contained in this resolution will we be able to be understanding.

Mr. President, I would like to convey to President Buzek the pride I feel at this moment in belonging to an important institution such as the European Parliament, which you are presiding over.
I believe that the sacrifices made over the past twenty years by your country, which today enable those twelve nations discussed earlier in this Assembly to be represented, lend strength to Europe.
However, I also wish to convey my concern upon hearing her program within her speech, which should, and I sincerely hope will, strengthen this Parliament.
I hope that what has been announced to us will come to pass.

I hope that the next President of the European Commission, certainly President Barroso, will be willing to listen to you, to the strong words you have spoken, in order to ensure that this Parliament provides answers to more than

The 550 million citizens in this Europe who have voted for us, who wanted us, who want us, and who demand from this Parliament, from all of us, the answers that Europe has certainly tried to provide, but has not fully succeeded in delivering.
In this endeavor of hers, I express and set in motion my hopes, and I am confident that with her we shall achieve the certainties she herself has bestowed upon us.

Mr. President, Commissioner, esteemed colleagues, it is neither my intention, nor that of my group, which has supported and wished for this debate, to transfer competences in Italian internal policy to the European level.

The issue of freedom of information concerns us all; it concerns the European Union, which can and must regulate the matter at the community level, in order to counter political and economic pressures and ensure genuine pluralism in information.

The Italian anomaly, where the prime minister also owns some of the country's most important private media networks, lies precisely in the dangerous entanglement among media, political, and economic powers.

It is precisely for this reason that we call for a European regulatory intervention, an intervention that, far from being an act of anti-Italian sentiment, would serve to safeguard the pluralism of information, preventing concentrations and conflicts of interest.

For this reason, today we urge the Commission to act as soon as possible, as this Parliament has also requested in the past, to equip the European Union with common standards safeguarding the freedom of information for all European citizens.
Mr. President, honourable colleagues, I am aware that on 30 September the Commission presented a study on media pluralism indices in the Member States.
However, in 2008 a report was adopted, drafted by Marianne Micko, which invited the European Commission and the Member States to safeguard, verbatim, the diversity of resources.
on media opinions, to defend the pluralism of information, to ensure all citizens of the European Union equal access to free and diverse information media.

Earlier, in 2004, a report was adopted in Parliament, drafted by rapporteur Bogert Kvack, on the risks of infringement of freedom of expression and information within the European Union, in which the Commission was requested to submit a communication on ensuring media pluralism in all member states.
To date, the Commission has still not provided clear answers to the Parliament's requests.

You will note, Madam Commissioner, that I have not mentioned my own country or my Prime Minister, but here, as a European citizen, I ask you what the Commission intends to do at the European level to ensure media pluralism is guaranteed in all Member States.
Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the speech regarding yesterday’s vote in the Environment Committee has been repeated many times.
I would like it to be clear, rather, that not everything can be reduced to a single extremist and ideological stance.

Disasters and Europe should in this regard be a role model and not repeat the same mistakes, even if dressed in different clothes and bearing labels that appear seemingly much friendlier.

I must recall that the very same emissions trading directive provides that the European Union's virtuous pathway shall be subject to evaluation by the Commission based on the outcomes of the conference.
At the conference, we must go in with firmly established positions and clear ideas, but we must absolutely strive to fairly balance the interests.

We must also support efforts among all industrialized countries, first of all by setting equivalent emission reduction targets, but also without forgetting what our colleague Grossetête reminded us so clearly, which is now the central theme of our discussion.

But among developing countries, we cannot avoid making distinctions—there are developing countries, truly, and then there are countries that already have emerging economies, such as India, China, and Brazil.

These are key points: we want the Kyoto Protocol itself to retain its environmental significance, and we especially want to ensure that no financial bubble is created at the expense of our European enterprises.
It is very important for two reasons: the first is the research topic, which concerns the fight against neurodegenerative diseases, and the second is the mode of joint research programming.

If we must now proceed without halting the process, we nevertheless request assurances that our opinion will be taken into account in the Council's competitiveness meeting, and that a clearer legal basis will be established for the future, ensuring full parliamentary involvement and greater parliamentary ownership in this field of research.

We must now act with appropriate means and resources to prevent and eradicate such widespread afflictions as Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, and other diseases that are expected to increase with the aging of the population.
We call for a focus on large-scale, extensive studies, whether directed toward diagnosis or the identification of treatments.

Fundamental appear to be research on biomarkers, diagnostic methods for early detection based on a multidisciplinary approach, the establishment of extensive data banks, the search for curative drugs, and the development of appropriate models of care and services.

At this juncture, I feel compelled to emphasize only one point: neither the patients' subjectivity—too often excluded in this type of illness—nor the involvement of patient and family associations should be overlooked.
From a methodological standpoint, we consider joint research projects to be very important because they address a fundamental need: the concentration of efforts and resources.
overcome those fragmentations, those duplications, and thus be able to achieve a sufficient critical mass to yield appropriate outcomes for this kind of research.

on strategic lines and shared programs between the states and Europe, and on common initiatives among public and private actors, without overlooking the international framework that connects us with major scientific entities worldwide.
What are the innovations of the Stockholm Programme compared to the EU's previous strategy, a fair balance between rights and security, and legal protection in both criminal and civil matters?

Safety is a right not to be attacked in one's place of work, to walk the streets without risking one's life, to not be subjected to violence simply because one is a woman, and to not be exposed to acts of terrorism.
The states and Europe must ensure security.
But a security measure that weakens the guarantees of freedom is one that undermines the certainty of law, and thus becomes a source of insecurity and barbarism.
Guantanamo.
Europe is the homeland of rights.
The Stockholm Programme defines them in great detail.

Today, a fair balance must be maintained between the discipline of criminal law enforcement on one hand, and the daily lives of citizens, their family, social, economic, professional, and academic relationships— all governed by civil law and civil justice.
This is the Europe of the citizen.
It must be built within the states and within the community institutions.
European society is more united, and mobility more intense, than commonly believed.
Mobility is now a right.
State conflicts are not impermeable walls; they are networks through which society filters everything daily.
The first is that Stockholm provides the institutional framework for judicial cooperation, mutual recognition, a European justice, and national justice.
It is the community-based one, a European, national, and community right, a European citizen, a European judge, as well as a national and a community judge.

European rules are established by community regulations and the case law of European courts, but also stem from the conduct of workers, businesses, students, judges—including national judges—and European networks among justice professionals.
Bottom-up, therefore, and this grassroots process, the Stockholm Programme intends to support it.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, we were in Ramallah when we received the news that Europe had adopted a new decision regarding the peace process in the Middle East.
We have seen the smile of hope on the faces of the Palestinian representatives.

They were intense days filled with emotions and suffering, etched into the faces of children clinging to the grilles of the windows of their homes, on a street in Hebron—the ghost street that no Palestinian may cross.

The suffering of an 85-year-old woman evicted from her occupied home, forced to sleep in the cold in a tent placed in the garden of the house she had once lived in, the suffering visible on the faces of those Palestinians who had built a farm with European funding, only to see it destroyed by Israeli soldiers.
Israel has the right to be an independent state, but Palestinians also have the right to have a homeland, a state, and a passport.
The Council of Europe's decision represents a significant step forward, but immediate action is required.
The conflict between Israel and Palestine is the tip of the iceberg of a larger conflict.
On the final day, Israeli authorities prevented us, the European Parliament delegation, from visiting the Gaza Strip.
I believe that on this issue, the High Representative, Baroness Ashton, should intervene to express the protest of democratic Europe.

I thank President De Rossa, who led the delegation, and all the other members of the delegation, because during the days we spent in Palestine, the vulnerable people of Palestine were able to see and hear the face and words of an Europe that defends rights and wants, and must, do more.
Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the European Union is committed to combating terrorism in all its dimensions.

When discussing the fight against terrorist crimes, it is essential to ensure that fundamental rights are fully respected and that the measures adopted to combat terrorism are appropriate and effective.

The rights of defence and fundamental procedural guarantees must therefore be fully respected by the institutions of the Union, even when they establish lists of persons and entities, in this case linked to someone.

Al Qaeda, subject to restrictive measures, and it is equally essential that these measures be subject to adequate democratic and parliamentary oversight, as rightly prescribed by the Treaty of Lisbon.

It is therefore clear, as also stated by the Parliament's legal service, that any measure of this kind must follow the co-decision procedure, thereby enabling representatives of European citizens to fully perform their legislative and oversight roles.
Mr. President, I would like to avoid continuing to list the reasons for our disagreement with the Iranian government.

Although the Iranian government has signed the United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child, it continues to impose capital punishment on minors.

European attention on the affairs of Iran, and, on the other hand, that the interparliamentary commission with the Iranian government acknowledges that a change in course is required.

I would like the Commission from this moment onward to turn a new page and establish relationships of assistance, support, and dialogue with representatives of the opposition in exile, not least of which is Mrs. Miriam Rajavi, who embodies this reality.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the issue of fiscal good governance, while always of great importance, has taken on even greater relevance and urgency following the major economic and financial crisis of two years ago.
They have discussed it and continue to discuss it, international and European leaders, particularly when addressing the fight against tax evasion and tax havens.
All of this is important, a sign of commitment and determination, but one must not delude oneself into thinking that an announcement alone is sufficient.
A serious and continuous policy is required.
Many problems remain.
Is it really still too easy to buy or set up a ghost company in order to evade taxes?
Just browse the internet for a moment—there are thousands of websites offering companies for purchase, even in European Union countries.
Often, it is sufficient to send an email with a scanned copy of the passport attached in order to open a company.
We must put an end to the practice of creating fictitious legal entities to evade taxation.
This report, which I am presenting, is based on the European Commission's communication on fiscal governance of 28 April 2009.
The report moves in the direction of putting forward concrete proposals and calls for a strong commitment from the European Commission and the Council to implement these proposals.
The fight against tax havens, tax evasion, and illicit capital flight must be prioritized by the European Union.
From here stems the principle of good governance, founded on transparency, exchange of information, cross-border cooperation, and fair tax competition.
The point is that ever greater tax cooperation and collaboration are needed within the European Union.
The overarching goal we must set for ourselves is to achieve a global, multilateral automatic exchange of information, starting, of course, with the European Union.
The banking secrecy must be entirely abolished in European Union countries, and the derogation regime under these agreements must be brought to an end without further delay.
a temporary measure that allows for the application of a withholding tax at source, often evaded or underreported, instead of information exchange.
I do not wish to dwell on the specific proposals contained in the report.
I emphasize that we nonetheless need certain points, in particular, to expand the scope of application of the 2003 Savings Directive, combat VAT fraud, and establish a public register of the Union.
The Commission has furthermore proposed establishing a monitoring system that takes into account individuals or companies that have established corporations or opened accounts in tax havens.

Moreover, the European Union must speak with one voice in international forums, actively advocating for the improvement of OECD standards to achieve automatic exchange of information, rather than exchange upon request.

Mr. Commissioner Kovac, please also take into account the other reports; we need a strong commitment from the Commission, and at this moment, these priorities need to be emphasized for the new Commission at the time of the handover.
We will hold the Council and the European Commission accountable for their work.
I thank my colleagues, and above all the shadow rapporteurs, for their contribution to this work, which I sincerely hope will be approved by Parliament.

Mr. President, I must admit that when we speak of combating terrorism, discussing only intelligence and technology, I always have the impression that we are inadequately addressing the issue, as though we were treating a disease by focusing solely on its symptoms rather than its underlying causes.
Is it not high time we addressed the problem of terrorism by targeting its root causes, rather than confining ourselves to its external manifestations?

Addressing the heart of the problem means, for example, becoming aware that terrorism is also a direct manifestation of the inability or unwillingness of ethnic groups to integrate into the European cultural fabric.
I wonder—and I ask this Parliament—do we want to discuss terrorism?
Let this Parliament therefore have the courage to politically address a debate on the degree of integration of Muslims in Europe and their willingness to share Western values, rights, and freedoms.
On this matter, I have submitted a written question to the Commission on the full veil to encourage this Assembly to engage openly with the issue.
It seems to me that there is no willingness whatsoever to address the issue.
I wonder, and I ask you, honourable colleagues from all political groups, if not on these issues, on what other matters should the political voice of the peoples of Europe be expressed?

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, closing the negotiation with Croatia by 2010 is an achievable goal, and the report fulfils the task of balancedly highlighting both the progress made and the steps still to be taken.
Now, justice is the sector in which further impactful reforms are still needed.
It is not sufficient, indeed, to complete the necessary systemic reforms, enact new laws, and cooperate as required with the International Criminal Tribunal for the former Yugoslavia.
I emphasize the necessity of establishing a genuine legal culture and mindset in line with European standards.

The independence of the judiciary, a central issue, the training, recruitment, and career progression of judges, the absence, that is, of various types of pressures exerted by the government on judges, become crucial.
I appeal to the Commission to consider the need for decisive measures in this regard during the final phase of the negotiation.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, Croatia's accession to the European Union strengthened the development of a European identity capable of expressing the shared values of the new Europe without obscuring, but rather integrating, the specific characteristics of its many diverse souls.

The Republic of Croatia has made commendable efforts in aligning with the required criteria, particularly in combating organized crime through new anti-mafia measures, but it needs to undertake further efforts, especially in the judicial sector, before the 2010 accession negotiations can be concluded.

Additional progress can be achieved by the institutions of the Republic of Croatia in aligning themselves with the provisions of the first protocol to the European Convention on Human Rights, signed in Paris in 1952, by assessing the restitution of nationalized property to its rightful owners.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, yet again, recently, an environmental disaster has occurred in Italy, along the Lambro River and the Po River.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, this debate is not ceremonial; it aims to be very concrete, posing clear and precise questions about the resources needed and where they should come from. I hope, and the Parliament hopes, that the responses today and in the upcoming Councils of 12 and 25 March will be equally clear and precise.

We wish to firmly tell the Commission and the Council that the European Union, which has raised before the world the banner of the fight against atmospheric emissions and the climate challenge, today has the duty of consistency, if it does not wish to lose credibility.
It must do everything possible to achieve these objectives.

We must clearly understand that discussing a low-emission economy amounts to a Copernican revolution for the productive system, meaning we must change many things, establish clear priorities, demonstrate political will, but above all possess adequate resources and tools.
We also know that this Copernican revolution is essential in order for our productive system, European industry, to remain competitive.

I mean that in many countries, in numerous research centers, and in many universities, the intellects are ready, the capabilities are present, the will is there—what is now needed is to ignite this engine.
They must all do it, the Commissioner said, the Council said, the member states must do it, entrepreneurs must do it, the private sector must do it.
But I believe the indispensable task of bringing together this complex partnership lies with the European Union.
To address the employment issue in a constructive manner, the guiding principles in community actions are threefold: react, evolve, and protect.

React, that is, abandon the celebratory tone of euro-propaganda and address in a concrete manner the problems affecting EU citizens, ensuring a direct link between the world of education and the needs of the market and local communities, and requalifying young people's perception of manual professions.

To evolve, that is, to turn the EU 2020 strategy into a positive synthesis between the demands of businesses and those of workers, in favor of a labor market where the flexibility of contractual forms responds to the instability of market demand.

To protect, meaning to provide work for those who have lost their jobs, first and foremost for our own citizens. Member States must therefore assess immigration policies according to the actual need for immigration, and with 23 million unemployed, one should consider the possibility of suspending extra-community immigration for several years.
I do not see any other way to protect our work and our social system.
Concrete action, courage, and decentralization are required.
Mr. President, this is an oral amendment to point 13, calling on the Commission to assess, in the current situation, the possibility of providing verified humanitarian assistance to the situation.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, I would like to once again emphasize the importance of the written declaration on corruption that I have proposed together with four other Members of Parliament, which has been approved by a majority of more than half of the European Parliament, thereby obliging the Council and the Commission to adopt truly effective legislation on corruption.

I have returned, on behalf of the Budgetary Control Committee, from a visit to Greece, and undoubtedly one of the reasons behind Greece's structural crisis also stems from the wasteful use of public funds—a situation that also occurs in Italy.

Corroded by corruption, as emerges in these days—corruption primarily revolving around the illegal management of public funds, the relationship between a segment of politics and a segment of entrepreneurs, and organized white-collar crime.

It is essential that the European Parliament exert full pressure on the Commission and on the Member States, that Member States failing to comply be sanctioned, and that European institutions responsible for monitoring corruption and combating organized crime be strengthened.
In male and female relationships, we well know that this difference is even more pronounced in the context of self-employment activities.

To address this issue, I believe it is necessary to implement measures such as those contained in the directive we are discussing, while keeping firmly in view the polestar of the family. Only in this way can these measures acquire meaning and offer hope of success.

Releasing women from the narrow dilemma of choosing between the roles of mother, wife, and entrepreneur means alleviating the burden of household work and directly addressing concrete issues through positive, specific measures supporting the family.
Putting the family at the center; otherwise, any package of measures will be a disjointed set of interventions, ultimately useless.

It involves initiating a virtuous process that addresses the serious imbalance between the need for organs and their available supply, without undermining the principle of free and voluntary donation.

any form of illegal commercialization and trafficking, ensuring the quality and safety of organs intended for transplantation, through measures that effectively balance confidentiality with traceability.

On this subject, I believe it would be very important to establish a European database and adopt common quality and safety standards.
We must be careful not to introduce unnecessary forms of rigidity or create additional bureaucratic burdens that could undermine what is currently being carried out correctly and effectively.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, I thank the rapporteur for the excellent work carried out on the European Union's accession to the Convention on Human Rights, which has reached its first agreement in two years.

It represents a benefit for European citizens, as it will establish a new court outside the European Union to ensure that the rights of European citizens are consistently upheld by the European Union and its member states.

Under the principle of democracy, the European Union and its member states must always have the right to defend themselves; for this reason, I believe it is essential that every nation adhering to the convention must have a judge who explains the context of each appeal.

I believe the European Parliament has an informal body aimed at coordinating the exchange of information between the Parliament and the Parliamentary Assembly of the Council, and it is important that the Parliament also be consulted and involved in the negotiation process.
I further add that European citizens have the right to understand the mechanisms of this accession, just as they have the right to know what their rights are.

Therefore, I consider it essential that the Council of Europe and the European Union take into consideration the development of guidelines, with a clear explanation of all the effects and implications that membership will entail.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, 170 measures in five years—figures far too ambitious for a program lacking a genuine character, especially on certain issues.

On immigration, the program, beyond the official documents, reveals an embarrassing weakness: what is the point of promising and strengthening institutions and agencies if there is no underlying political strategy?

A truly effective strategy must be based on several key principles: combating clandestine immigration along the southern border, full solidarity among all European Union member states against clandestinity, a policy of agreements with third countries, and above all, recognizing that immigration, at a time of crisis, is not a resource.

One must instead reason with pragmatism and realism: today, the priority is providing jobs for our citizens; everything else is empty rhetoric that helps neither the integration of immigrants nor, least of all, our own people.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, as we know, the European Parliament is called upon to give its final opinion on food information for consumers.
It must be immediately stated that this was an objectively arduous, lengthy, and controversial legislative process that could not have been shortened.

It is indeed a matter of harmonizing Community law on food through regulations designed to ensure transparent information for consumers, thereby preventing them from making confused choices or decisions potentially harmful to their health.

The report by the honourable Sommer, to which recognition is due for the effort made to synthesize multiple requirements, therefore aims to address disputes and the various existing information systems across the Member States of the Union.

It is no coincidence that national regulations to date have differed in the terminology used to describe the nature of the regulations offered for sale, resulting in a system that varies from country to country, and fostering unfair competition at the expense of potential consumers.
Thus, based on the European Commission's proposal, a new form has been given to existing legislation, involving food industries on one hand and consumers on the other.
At the same time, the report urges the European food industry to clarify mandatory labelling requirements, as well as the indication and presentation of nutritional and food values.

In a globalized market, the European Union could not avoid the necessary innovation of food product legislation, in order to better protect food trade and safeguard against an increasingly invasive and uncontrolled international competition.

It is not new that we increasingly hear about the dangers posed by food products that, through teletransportation, carry labels and indications which often fail to meet food safety requirements, yet are still marketed as foods and beneficial to human health.
Madam Commissioner, we have requested this oral question and a parliamentary resolution in order to make clear to the Commission that a long-term solution is not possible.
Within the framework of the Stockholm Programme, it is of great importance to build a widespread European legal culture across our territory.

of these future projects, because we do not arrive too late, when the projects are already sufficiently formulated.

thus fostering a European awareness among all those living on these territories, so that they may experience firsthand the rights of European citizens.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the proposal on passengers' rights for bus travel aims to improve and protect passengers, particularly those with disabilities or reduced mobility.
This is the first regulation on passenger rights for bus travel and should ensure passengers, as is already the case in the rail and air sectors.
The position in second reading aims to establish a set of rights for passengers who choose to travel by bus.

I agree with the rapporteur Cancián's position, whom I thank for the excellent work accomplished, and I believe the scope of application of the regulation should be revised by excluding regional services when they are integrated with urban and suburban services.
It would be fair and appropriate to ensure passengers' rights in the event of accidents, cancellations, or departure delays, as had already been decided by the Parliament in its first reading.

Equally important is the issue of passenger rights information. It would be useful, indeed, to provide passengers with information regarding connections with other modes of transport, as this will also facilitate communication between passenger transport services by bus and those by rail.

With regard to disabled persons, individuals with reduced mobility must be afforded the highest level of protection, and I propose using a 24-hour period instead of 48 hours to request assistance, as well as stronger guarantees for compensation and replacement of mobility aids in the event of loss or damage.
Moreover, taking into account the needs of passengers with reduced mobility, any architectural barriers should be removed and existing infrastructure should be improved to ensure accessibility.
This regulation aims to enhance the competitiveness of the bus sector, promote intermodality, and above all, improve passengers' travel conditions.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, I thank the rapporteur, Sterckx, for having carried out excellent work on this report, which addresses how to extend free circulation in the maritime sector for navigation within the European Union.
I share the rapporteur's objective with regard to this directive, namely to exempt all maritime freight transports within the European Union from unnecessary administrative formalities.

Indeed, to achieve a European maritime space without borders, it is not only important that the various competent authorities cooperate satisfactorily, but also that the different simplification systems be applied simultaneously.
This is necessary to ensure the proper functioning of the entire European borderless maritime area, making sea freight transport more attractive and enabling its optimal use.

Harmonizing administrative procedures among Member States will enable smoother navigation between the various ports of the European Union and will revitalize maritime freight traffic, which currently accounts for less than 10 percent of the total.
To date, navigation continues to be hindered by the administrative formalities imposed upon it.
Electronic systems should therefore be brought into operation as quickly as possible, as this would enable a faster data exchange system.

Moreover, interoperability is crucial for achieving genuine administrative simplification, as the transmission of information via email is not entirely efficient and proves futile if information systems are not technically interoperable.
Regarding language, I believe that the use of a common language would certainly benefit European maritime navigation.
In this way, communication would be more efficient, causing less confusion and fewer administrative delays.

I genuinely hope that the outcomes of this reform immediately lead to a reduction in administrative burdens and, in the medium term, improved environmental performance and strengthened European industry.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the European Council, which no one has voted for, today also has a rotating presidency in the hands of a government discredited by its own citizens.

This alone would be enough to understand why Europe is experiencing a profound crisis, a Europe that has now become a political paradox in the hands of a political elite with which citizens no longer identify.
The program of the Belgian non-presidency refers to structural reforms, employment growth, financial stability, and competitiveness, with the chosen instrument being the EU 2020 strategy.
The current crisis should, however, urge Europe to adopt ingredients fundamentally different from those that led to the previous failure.

A fundamental reconsideration is required, because the crisis is not a mirror of decline, but rather the death certificate of the European social model that euro-propaganda would instead prescribe as a cure.
Today, Europe is a vast edifice collapsing under its own weight due to democracy, centralism, and the obstacles to free enterprise.
To overcome the crisis, Europe must change its vocabulary—more decentralization, less bureaucracy, greater concreteness, and greater attention to the real problems faced by citizens.
Everything else will only inexorably accelerate the decline.
I would like to emphasize in this intervention how the progressive erosion of European farmers' incomes, in light of the growing challenges in agriculture, represents an opportunity for the future.

The profit margins within the agro-industrial chain are creating a concerning economic marginalization of those employed in the agricultural sector, leading to consequent withdrawals from these activities.
A shift in direction is therefore required, a fact acknowledged by the European Commission itself and reflected in the report under examination by the House this morning.
The Chamber's approval this morning, which I wish to express my support for, underscores, with ample justification and suggestions of a legislative and sector-specific nature.
It is indeed necessary to fight against global speculation on raw materials and to ensure the security of supplies.

Moreover, improved price transparency must be ensured, not only to do justice to farming professionals but also to correct the significant imbalances in negotiating power among different operators and to prevent abuses across the supply chain.
Agriculture must reaffirm itself as a sector of economic and social stability and regain its influence within the commercial context of the European Union.
Mobilism and myopia—this is the diagnosis of the malaise affecting today's Europe 27.

Stagnation, because in Italy one in four young people is unemployed, and there are 16 million unemployed across the eurozone, precisely because the noise of euro propaganda over recent months has been followed by no concretely effective action.

Myopia because, in defining the way out of the crisis, Europe not only presented the usual recipe but also entirely overlooked the most authentically territorial dimension of economic and social cohesion policy.

Only by placing the territory at the heart of political action, and by respecting its distinctive cultural vocations, can European development and employment policies be revitalized, drawing upon the innate energies expressed by each geographical area through its districts, small and medium-sized enterprises, and craftsmanship.
To address the employment issue, one must first think small, investing in local bodies and promoting subsidiarity, while safeguarding a heritage composed of linguistic and cultural diversity.
If the needle of the European Brussels does not steer its policy toward the territory, the shipwreck of the europroject is a certainty.
Mr. President, in the construction of an Europe of rights, a central place must be given to freedom of information, pluralism of information, and independence of information.
Has the gentleman Commissioner, the lady Commissioner, noticed that there is an EU country—indeed, one of the founding ones, by the Treaty of Rome—where there is no
It is the pluralism of information, where a prime minister directly or indirectly controls public and private television as well as print media.
What democratic electoral competition can be tolerated in a country where a president, a council, and a political faction control the media?
What kind of democracy can exist in a country where a law is about to be passed shortly that prevents journalists from publishing facts related to judicial news?
Rather than Watergate, rather than a press serving as guardian of the regime, a lapdog for the drawing room—what kind of democracy is it where the Internet is to be brought under control?
What democratic country is this, where those who oppose the dominant ideology are sidelined, and where free expression of thought and dissent are not guaranteed?

We are not describing the Sudan of Bocassa, the Romania of Ceaușescu, or Italy under Berlusconi, where unfortunately this occurs, partly because the European Commission lacks the courage to adopt an independent and free stance vis-à-vis the member states.
Completing the internal market for postal services by the end of this year is an important step, but it would be a further step forward for growth.

It is appropriate that the Commission carry out an impact assessment, as some of my fellow speakers have reminded us, following the opening of the postal market, in order to better understand citizens' needs.

Indeed, how does the Commission intend to safeguard the right of European citizens living in hard-to-reach areas, such as mountainous or rural regions, to receive mail regularly, and what tools does the Commission intend to adopt to ensure this?

Madam President, Madam Commissioner, colleagues, today’s debate on readmission agreements cannot avoid the increasingly delicate issue concerning the agreement between the European Union and Libya.
We know that one aspect of the agreement is focused on combating clandestine immigration.

We are concerned because, until now, the Commission has provided us with little information on this matter, despite the Treaty of Lisbon obliging the Commission itself to promptly inform the Parliament.
As is well known, Libya has not ratified the Geneva Convention on Human Rights, nor has it, to date, demonstrated conduct consistent with international law.

I think of the case of Eritrean migrants imprisoned in inhumane conditions in the Libyan detention center of Brak, or of the closure of UNHCR offices, but above all I think of what happened last week, when a patrol boat of the Libyan Republic opened fire on a vessel off the Libyan coast.

A very serious justification, which leads us to consider the agreement signed between Libya and Italy—leading to the invasion of Libya—as an act of violence against an Italian fishing vessel from the port of Mazzara del Vallo, present in international waters.

Among other things, it provides for the return of immigrants to Libya without provisions regarding the right to apply for asylum or individual case-by-case assessments of each situation, and even includes the possibility of using firearms to stop immigrants.

Since in this case part of the agreement is also confidential, I wonder whether the Commission is aware of its content, and whether it does not consider it necessary, in this specific instance, to request explanations regarding the substance of this agreement.

It is an opportunity to recall that Libyan authorities have the possibility to open fire on vessels if there is suspicion that they are carrying irregular migrants, as otherwise this provision would be ineffective.

From an economic standpoint, it is positive for Europe to deepen commercial relations with neighboring countries experiencing strong growth; however, the EU has consistently pursued, at least in words, a trade policy toward third countries that takes into account social and humanitarian aspects.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, Commissioner, with this measure, the aim is to strengthen and rationalize the European Union's pharmacovigilance system.
These are the ambitious objectives set forth in the McAvan reports, which respectively amend Regulation 726 and Directive 2001.

This was made possible through intensive and efficient coordination work between the Council and the European Parliament, as well as between the relevant parliamentary committees with competence on the subject.
to arrive today at discussing a proposal aimed at improving the Community pharmacovigilance system and refining the substantial and decades-long activity of the European Union in this field.

Simplification, transparency, and rationalization can be regarded as the key elements of these two reports, which propose measures aimed at ensuring the highest possible protection of public health based on harmonized standards across Europe.

Indeed, reference is made to a broad range of measures, ranging from a clearer definition of the roles and responsibilities of the main national and supranational stakeholders involved, to a new and more consciously active involvement of the patient, to whom the Commission is addressing its appeal.

The Commission acknowledges a fundamental role for the actor in the system in strengthening the transparency of information regarding medicinal product safety, and in simplifying communication, registration, and assessment activities related to suspected adverse effects.
The current pharmacovigilance system must be improved through closer collaboration at the European, national, and regional levels.
Effective participation at all three of these levels will thus ensure a beneficial exchange of information regarding drug side effects.
The new pharmacovigilance system will be able to ensure a high level of shared and secure information in favor of patients, national authorities, and healthcare professionals.

The economic crisis that has struck our societies has made it harder and more difficult for everyone to provide appropriate responses to a new and serious social issue.
In this context, I believe everyone has understood that the cohesion policy has, on many occasions, been the only tangible instrument available to regions for attempting to provide responses.
We can therefore say that, in recent years, cohesion policy has been an essential lever for all regions in the process of European integration.
We are now redefining this policy for after 2013; we are therefore at a turning point.
For this reason, it is vital to avoid certain risks.
The first fundamental point is that of its renationalization; that would be a serious step backward.

Subsequently, it is necessary to ensure, in the construction of the funding budget, an appropriate balance both in favor of less developed regions and simultaneously in the ability to avoid penalizing the more competitive and advanced regions.
Finally, it is essential to streamline procedures for those who have demonstrated efficiency, and to strengthen the capacity to impose sanctions on those who have not.
We expect the Commission to proceed in this direction.
Mr. President, honorable colleagues, the path toward an Europe designed for women remains long and difficult.
The statistics speak clearly: the global crisis has hit the labor market hard, putting female employment under severe strain, which further declined by 0.7 percentage points in 2008.

Despite the recurring provocations on the topics of equal opportunities and employment that periodically fill the euro-propaganda aimed at securing consensus, concrete measures to ensure women's genuine fulfillment as workers and as mothers have still not been implemented to date.
The Europe of the future needs to radically rethink its welfare model, not merely change its label periodically.

Well, the directive that aligns across Europe the institution of maternity leave; however, the choice to address, on the fringes of this subject, in the same report the complementary and equally important issue of paternity leave is inefficient.

The greater integration of women is not merely a moral value, but also a strategic objective for the sustainability of the much-publicized European social model, which continues to fail to convince me due to its lack of results.

During 2009, the European Commission presented a proposal to amend Regulation 1905, which establishes a financial instrument for development cooperation.
With this proposal, the Commission had asked Parliament to approve an amendment enabling non-governmental organizations to benefit from tax advantages when operating in developing countries.
proposed by us and accepted.
However, the Cooperation and Development Regulation also contains the implementing rules for the European Union's development policy.
These rules provide that, when the Commission is required to allocate funding, it must follow comitology procedures.

This means that the Parliament has the authority to examine these funding proposals, and if the Commission exceeds its competences, the Parliament may then adopt resolutions requesting the Commission to revise the decisions in question.

Since 2006, the Parliament has considered that the Commission exceeded its executive competences in at least twelve cases, but in only three of these cases did the Commission actually modify or withdraw its proposal for decision.
Following the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon, the Committee on Development proposed applying the delegated acts procedure provided for in Article 290 of the Treaty of Lisbon.
The adoption of these procedures would mean that, at least in strategic funding decisions that the European Commission is required to take, the Parliament would have a more significant role.
Indeed, according to the Treaty of Lisbon, in certain clearly defined cases the Parliament may delegate to the Commission the power to take strategic decisions, but what might these decisions be?
In our opinion, the selection of countries to which the European Union should provide development assistance

It is a choice that falls to the legislator, and then, which sectors should be prioritized in funding: education, health, environmental protection, effective governance, or the development of small enterprises?
And how can transparency in the management of development aid be ensured?
These are the areas and issues in which the European Parliament should play a more significant role than it has in the past.
It is here that the legislator must provide the executive with clear guidance.
And this is precisely the direction, I believe, toward which the demands of European citizens ultimately point.
I truly hope for the widest possible support for the honourable Mitchell's report.

governance mechanisms, participatory both at the inter-institutional level and in international cooperation for regional basins, with particular emphasis on the Mediterranean due to the complexity of its jurisdiction.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, I thank the honourable Meissner for her report on integrated maritime policy. I agree with the rapporteur on the need to strengthen maritime transport and shipbuilding, to protect coastal regions, and to promote innovation and research activities.
Today, maritime transport plays a fundamental role in the commercial sector.
For this reason, cooperation between small and large ports overlooking the same sea basin should be encouraged, as they can facilitate a more fluid commercial exchange.
Moreover, to ensure intermodality, investment is needed in port and hinterland infrastructure, particularly for ports that can be connected to European corridors.
Finally, I believe it is important not to overlook another issue, namely port security.

It is necessary to achieve homogenization of all security measures across all European ports, in order to better protect maritime transport and prevent unfair competition against those ports that, in order to ensure greater security, must incur higher costs.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, thirty years ago Morocco denied for many years that there was a war with the Sahrawi people; today it denies that there is disproportionate repression against the same people.
Yet in Western Sahara there are dead and wounded among a population that claims nothing more than the right to live in freedom on its own land.
Morocco has essentially closed its borders to anyone who might be a witness to these events.
No information and no deputies.
A French deputy and our colleague Mayer have been denied access.
Europe watches passively.
There is no sign of the Commission's communication that makes us subordinate to United Nations mediation, which continues to fail as it has over the past thirty years.
There is no statement from Ms. Ashton, who is responsible for foreign policy.
Action must be taken through a statement of position by the Parliament.
Action must be taken against Morocco by suspending all initiatives regarding trade agreements, starting with the renewal of fishing agreements.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, my intervention is not meant to be the usual rhetorical exercise of euro-scepticism, but rather a call for the EU to focus more on substance than on form, and to abandon propaganda in favour of concreteness.
Having failed the Lisbon strategy, Europe has devised the new slogan EU 2020, but in practice has provided no innovative response to employment issues.

The data confirm it: 9.6% unemployment rate in Europe, 20.2% among youth. In Italy, the third net contributor to the EU budget, the situation for young people is even more dramatic, with 25.9% of youths unemployed.

In the Europe I would like to see, there are no waste of resources in promotional campaigns, but efficient investments so that training offers prepare professionals for sectors of the internal market, where concrete job opportunities exist.
I conclude by emphasizing the importance of investing in professional training, a symbol of excellence in our regions.

The world of craftsmanship offers a unique potential for employment and a valuable heritage of knowledge, which must be entrusted with the task of supporting the competitiveness of the European system.

Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, Madam President of the Council, the European commitment to establishing ambitious targets ahead of the upcoming climate change conference is undoubtedly a goal to be pursued with determination and awareness that the outcomes will shape the future of our planet.
But precisely for this reason, dear colleagues, we must be realistic and more cautious in our expectations.

To our knowledge, the international negotiations leading up to the Cancun Conference have not only failed to achieve significant progress toward a global agreement, but have also reinforced mistrust and resistance toward the commitments made in Copenhagen.
It has already been mentioned, but I would also like to mention China here.
China allows itself, for example, an increase in its emissions of 5 billion tons of CO2 by 2020, with consequences that could potentially be offset
And perhaps not even that, but merely an attempt at compensation through a 100% reduction in European industrial emissions within the same year, by 2020.

This figure not only makes the proposal for an unconditional 30% reduction highly problematic, which we strongly oppose, but also leads many independent observers to assume that global emissions will rise significantly by that date.

In this contradictory situation, it would be at the very least risky, and even demagogic, to hypothesize specific figures or sketch suggestive but unrealistic scenarios, given that the uncertainties inevitably affect the scale of costs.

The hope is that the European Union will bring to Cancun a shared, realistic, and sustainable proposal, because an agreement delivering tangible and perceptible results is always preferable to a long-term agreement.

the expectations inevitably destined to failure and increasingly undermining credibility in the eyes of the international public opinion—this, indeed, would be a damage scarcely repairable, the efforts of governments on major environmental issues.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, I have only one regret this evening in this Chamber, that we are discussing the Sahrawi issue solely because the Moroccans have invaded the Tindouf camp outside El Aaiun, and this is a regret for democracy within this Parliament.
The Parliament should have independently developed the will to discuss this issue, which has been stagnating on international tables for thirty years.
This is the most significant political fact.
We have been compelled by acts of violence, not because we voluntarily chose to secure human rights.
Why, if not the respect for human rights, is it that there must be a referendum on self-determination there?
It is precisely because Morocco is our friend, because Morocco is the strong component of our relations. We must firmly and through all necessary actions demand an irrevocable will.

We must strive to create the conditions under which, Mr. President, the mission—thorough and self-contained—will not be left alone in establishing the grounds for a referendum that is continually postponed.
We must strive to use all the tools available to the European Union to influence this event and ensure that the referendum does justice to thirty years of suffering endured by a people.
Mr. President, apart from this issue, I would like to raise a matter with the Presidency.

I urge the Presidency to summon Commissioner Kallas to the House and explain the programme he intends to pursue in this extremely sensitive and important area for the European Parliament.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the report under discussion undoubtedly contains positive elements; the overall orientation is that of simplifying procedures and streamlining bureaucratic steps for workers from third countries holding a legal residence permit in a Member State.

However, as a point of reflection, I would like to recall that today, across Europe, tens of millions of citizens, including very many young people, are unemployed due to structural shortcomings in the European productive system and as a result of a crisis that appears to have no end.

Before addressing the needs of third-country nationals residing in our countries, Europe must especially commit to implementing economic, political, and social measures that help our citizens first of all find or regain employment.
If Europe succeeds in providing employment for its citizens, it will establish solid social foundations for adequately managing migratory flows from outside.
The key principle must first be to enable Europe to grow on its own legs and through its own strength.
And then there are the problems for our countries.
Mr. President, honourable colleagues, to date access to employment is regulated only for certain specific categories of citizens from third countries.
Indeed, not everyone benefits from the general principle of equal treatment in access to employment.

In this context, the European Union must act to ensure fair treatment for both third-country nationals legally residing within the Member States and for beneficiaries of the status of refugee or person in need of international protection, pursuant to Directive 2004/83/EC of 29 April 2004.
It is necessary to avoid an increasing demand for seasonal staff, for example, solely because they are paid less or have different, lower costs compared to a European citizen hired for the same job.

For instance, one must avoid the risk that many multinational companies relocate their headquarters to countries such as Morocco or Turkey and send their employees to work in their European branches because it is more cost-effective.
For reasons of social justice, ensuring equal treatment of European citizens in terms of remuneration, working conditions, and social security is essential.
For this reason, I consider it appropriate that seasonal workers, posted workers, refugees, and self-employed workers be included in this directive.
Making distinctions will become extremely dangerous.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, human trafficking, according to estimates by international organizations and police authorities, has now become the world's second-largest source of profit for organized crime, after drug trafficking.
It is estimated that around 30 million people are victims of organ trafficking, prostitution, and forced labor.
This means that, first and foremost, the fight against this crime of immense scale is a battle for the security of our citizens, against clandestinity, against crime that generates further crime.
Faced with a phenomenon of such proportions, Europe must rightly implement drastic measures against those who exploit human beings in various forms.
For this reason, in combating this phenomenon, cooperation among member countries and law enforcement agencies is essential, but above all, the certainty of penalties for traffickers.

They are not exploited because in their countries of origin they do not find decent living conditions, but instead live in poverty and despair, and are willing to do anything just to leave their homeland in search of a better life that ultimately will not materialize.

Europe, therefore, must commit itself more fully and with greater diligence not only to preventing crime and protecting victims of trafficking, but also to ensuring that these individuals can find acceptable living conditions in their home countries.
We must assist them in their own homes; this must be the guiding principle of European political action, so that our efforts are directed not only toward repression but also toward prevention.
It's a bit too much.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, as Vice-Chair of the Committee on Development, I am actively engaged with the issues affecting many countries, particularly in the African region, where human rights are regularly violated, trampled upon, and sometimes even forgotten.

I returned just last Sunday from an official mission to the Democratic Republic of the Congo, where, in addition to the customary institutional meetings, I made a point of meeting with representatives of the NGOs operating in the country and visiting some of the centers where they work.
I met the victims in a country where rape is conceived as a weapon of war, where armed groups continue to attack women and children.
repeatedly commit mass rapes and other systematic crimes against civilian populations, particularly against women, the elderly, and children.

The international community must significantly increase funding for initiatives aimed at protecting women and addressing other forms of violence.
