It can also be noted that this choice is auspicious for the new generations, who will increasingly share a common vision of the European heritage capable of historically and culturally uniting them.

I thank you for the rapporteur's efforts and extend my sincere congratulations on the negotiations conducted with the Commission, which have led to the successful implementation of this initiative, so crucial for the future of environmental preservation.
Mr. President, honourable colleagues, neurodegenerative diseases, including Alzheimer's disease in particular, represent a public health priority for the European Union.
The condition in question carries serious consequences, not only health-related for those who experience it.
However, the costs that must be incurred for direct and informal care are indeed high.
Alzheimer's disease, in fact, affects not only the individuals who suffer from it, but also involves all those who care for the patients themselves.

A European response to the issue is therefore necessary, a global coordination among the Member States, a synergy of concerted actions, particularly in the scientific and research fields, which must take on the responsibility of

enhancement of prevention, diagnosis, and improvement of available, including innovative, treatments, as well as the dissemination of information among Member States regarding achievements attained within each, incremental increases in citizens' awareness and knowledge of the disease.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the situation is that Campania, particularly the city of Naples, are now universally and unfortunately known throughout the world, and the European Union has had to address this issue on multiple occasions.

Faced with this already paradoxical third-world situation, I therefore ask the Commission what consequences Italy will face from the standpoint of community law, and what economic sanctions might be imposed on Italy if the rural waste crisis does not find a definitive solution in the near future.

I further request that the Commission provide detailed information on the European funds that have so far been managed by the Campania region for the establishment of an integrated waste collection and disposal system.
You see, Commissioner, usually when we speak of emergencies, we refer to dramatic, extreme situations that nonetheless find a resolution within reasonable time frames.
When a so-called emergency lasts for more than 15 years, evidently something is not working.
In recent years, the Italian government has done everything possible to address the longstanding issue of waste collection and disposal cycles.
The blame primarily lies with the local Campanian authorities and their complete inability—or lack of will—to take action in order to find a solution.

It is clear and absolutely beyond any doubt that the conditions in which Naples and Campania find themselves are due to the long-standing neglect exhibited by municipalities, provinces, and regions in managing the waste cycle.
If differentiated waste collection is not carried out, the first indispensable element of a functioning waste management plan, it cannot be blamed on the central government.

If the Italian government had to send in the army, that happened a few weeks ago, but on other occasions in the past as well, to replace hundreds of street cleaners who suddenly and simultaneously fell ill—clearly, this cannot be the fault of the central government.

If the emergency has affected Naples, while conditions elsewhere in Italy are very different—such as in my region, Veneto, where fruit collection has been carried out for years without problems and with an extremely high cycle rate—it means that the responsibility for the emergency lies

on Campania's politics and local administrators, and especially on the severe complicity with organized crime that has long sought and obtained enormous profits from the waste management business due to the Camorra's infiltration of politics and local administrations.
Following the lies of the Berlusconi government, which took office in 2008 telling Italians that the waste problem had been resolved, it is right that the European Union does so.
blocking funds to Italy until a compatible waste cycle is submitted, based on waste recycling and composting.

It is certainly not the plan presented by President Caldoro, which once again relies on uncontrolled landfills—such as those in Chiaiano, Taverna del Re, and Terzigno, even within the Vesuvius National Park—nor on incinerators that cause death, cancer, or a genuine public health emergency.
Public funds in the countryside, during the environmental emergency of these thirteen years, have consolidated criminal ties between politics—both left and right—and entrepreneurs and the Camorra.

And in environmental matters, dissent is resolved through the criminalization of opposition, through the criminalization of local communities, families, and the territory itself—as the Italian government has done by using the malganello to suppress those who defend nature and the land.

The international refugee is a person who, fearing persecution on account of race, religion, nationality, social group membership, or political opinion, is outside the country of which they are a national.
Cesare Battisti is not a political refugee.

Despite the protection France has offered for years, we are dealing with a serial killer, perpetrator or instigator of four murders, who has concealed himself behind the pretext of political struggle to evade life imprisonment.
It is shameful for a large country like Brazil to deny the extradition of a criminal who has never shown remorse, while granting him the status of political refugee.
Italian authorities, and above all the victims' families, demand to see this criminal brought to justice.
Faced with President Lula's insistence on blocking extradition, the EU cannot leave the Italian government to face the situation alone.
Europe must and should use all diplomatic tools at its disposal to ensure Italy obtains Battisti's extradition, even at the cost of threatening the suspension of cooperation agreements with Brazil.
to provide concrete support to the Tunisian people and their transitional government, and to revive an Euro-Mediterranean dialogue that has been strongly neglected in recent years.
The outburst of anger among the youth and the poor of the Maghreb and Egypt stems from the unjust distribution of wealth and the restrictions on the exercise of fundamental freedoms.

North Africa ignites, demanding greater democracy, economic development, participation, jobs, and gazes in astonishment at the old European continent, frozen in place, unable to grasp the social transformations taking place in that region or to develop genuine policies for progress and peace.
Europe should pay greater attention to human rights and democracy, promote more dialogue, more cooperation, and more tangible economic interventions.
Europe must open its doors and its heart to North Africa in order to make the Mediterranean a sea of peace.
Enough with rejecting immigrants; through violent cooperation with countries like Libya, more investments, more receptions, more aid, and a stronger Euro-Mediterranean policy.
It is demanded of us by history.
The resolution approved today by a majority on the waste emergency in Campania is a very important signal.
The Campania emergency becomes a European emergency, and it is a decision in favor of the citizens of Campania.

We say no to funding expansions that merely consolidate corrupt politicians, unscrupulous entrepreneurs, and the camorra; we say yes to European funds if a credible plan is presented, a credible plan.
An environmentally compatible waste management plan, no uncontrolled dumping in national parks, no incinerators producing dioxin, yes instead to recycling and environmentally sustainable methods.
And one also says no to the criminalization of local communities' dissent, thus yes to legality.

It is a significant turning point, a warning to the Berlusconi government and to the regional government led by Caldoro, which so far have done nothing but talk and entrench their cliques and the mafia-connected bourgeoisie.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the issue of falsified medicines is becoming an increasingly widespread problem on the international and community levels, both in terms of the rising number of cases and the growing sophistication of these counterfeits.
It is to these challenges that the report by the honourable Mathias seeks to respond, the outcome of long and painstaking negotiations.

The report, which we have expressed our support for, has established a regulatory framework that, starting from more precise and detailed definitions, has taken into account the needs of each country.
We must address all stakeholders, stages, and withdrawals within the pharmaceutical supply chain, ensuring safety, traceability, distribution chain obligations, and achieving a sound balance.
These are some of the aspects on which efforts must be made to achieve the first objective of establishing stricter regulations, in order to prevent the entry of new workers.
of counterfeit medicines, leading to a positive and more comprehensive regulatory framework capable of safeguarding citizens' health and protecting them from the risk of serious consequences.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, Egypt, from the virtual square of Facebook to the real one, with millions of people demanding a fairer distribution of wealth, greater justice, and protesting against corruption.

From the transition of soldiers and carabinieri, we must move toward genuine democracy: a new constitution, new elections, the abolition of the death penalty, more humane prisons, social rights, and freedoms.
It will be difficult.
Lurking in ambush are those who, in the name of an idea, believe they may employ any means.
We would like to believe in the wonderful Egyptian people, who have never however experienced democracy.

We want a secular democracy, with a clear separation between religious authority and political power, a democracy that embraces minorities and fulfills genuine rights—public healthcare, social security, employment.
Europe should support this path, but by investing resources capable of fostering genuine development in Egypt—no more dictatorships, no more poverty, no more deprivation.
Mr. President, Commissioner, I believe the issues concerning the Mediterranean have, with this measure, entered a phase even more pressing than the actions we have previously taken.
I believe that the act we are establishing today is far more important than what was enacted under the Community regulation of 2006.

I believe this also addresses some limitations of that regulation, which, being unilateral, has left large sectors of the fishing industry often convinced that Europe is doing its part, while other riparian countries are not.
I believe it is strategic for us to address this topic in this dimension and with these characteristics.
The work carried out by our colleague Rivellini, which has been widely appreciated, deserves a formal acknowledgment here.
Nevertheless, I have the distinct impression that the fact that we are unable to resolve this issue at first reading and will therefore proceed to the second reading
The report, particularly regarding the interpretation of Article 28 of this regulation, raises a matter on which the Parliament, on this occasion, sided with the Commission.

The Parliament essentially states that it is appropriate for these delegated acts to be under the Commission's responsibility, and there is some resistance from the Council, moreover, today's absence is also a significant one.
But this act has fostered in Parliament the idea that preserving biodiversity and maintaining the fish abundance of this important sea is also the key to the future of fishermen.
Having said that, I believe the phase ahead of us must centrally repropose the concept of co-decision.

These delegated acts have their own rationale for delegation and are therefore conferred today upon the Commission, but international agreements must ensure that the Parliament's role remains firmly secured when these take place.
Mr. President, Commissioner, in my opinion, we are facing two issues in this matter—one of methodology and the other substantive.

The issue with the method is that we are proceeding through a device for which we thank the honourable Fraga, namely oral question, in order to discuss something that should instead, following the Treaty of Lisbon, be a right of this Parliament.
I believe this practice must come to an end, and we cannot resort to a strategy aimed at supporting a co-decision process.
Co-decision occurs when it is cultivated in the stages in which it matures.
Today we are holding this debate; the treaty ends, it will continue, and we will say yes or no at the end.
Well, this method must be reconverted.

On the substance, however, I merely wished to emphasize—being in agreement with many points raised by my colleagues—the assessment of the impacts of fishing agreements, so that they are not merely commercial arrangements through which we simply purchase cephalopods at one euro per kilogram in Mauritania.

These indicators must be capable of measuring not only sustainability, but above all the impact on the economy of that country, understanding how many jobs are affected, assessing the level of innovation in artisanal fishing, and evaluating the use of these resources.
Not merely a simple commercial exchange, but a fishing agreement with all its implications.
The favorable vote expressed for the honourable Rivasi's report stems from an assessment of the management of the H1N1 influenza.
2001, which is the 2009 version aiming to establish general behavioral guidelines valid for any future occasions of global health emergency.
The report, following a long and positive phase of revision, establishes cooperation, independence, and transparency as its core principles.

It aims for greater and improved cooperation between the health authorities of the Member States and European institutions, in order to establish a harmonized and coordinated risk management approach.
The objectives are to demonstrate the Member States' strategy regarding national influenza management plans, including vaccine stockpiles and the publication of individuals' names.
expert institutes consulted by European health authorities and an effective trust-based relationship with media outlets responsible for disseminating public health messages.
That is why it received our favorable vote.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, up to today, the recommendations adopted by the General Committee for Fisheries in the Mediterranean have been incorporated into Community legislation provisionally, through annual fishing opportunities regulations.
These concern conservation, management, exploitation, control, and marketing measures for fisheries and aquaculture products in the Mediterranean.
The permanent nature of these recommendations, however, requires a more stable legal instrument for their incorporation into Community law.
It is therefore advisable to receive the aforementioned recommendations through a single legislative act, to which future recommendations may subsequently be added.
Today we have done this, and it will enhance legal certainty, while simultaneously representing an important step toward simplification.
At this point, the contracting parties to the CGMPM are obliged to ensure full implementation of the agreed measures starting from the established date.

A timely transposition was therefore necessary in order to make these international measures directly applicable to natural or legal persons at the community level and to ensure legal certainty in this area.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, I believe the approval of our colleague Podima's report represents a significant step forward towards a more balanced, more advanced, and stronger economic and fiscal policy at the European level.

It is now evident to all the urgency of equipping Europe, as a coordination mechanism among the twenty-seven countries, with economic and financial instruments capable of governing the economy, rather than merely succumbing to it.
For this reason, the proposal to tax financial transactions has generated significant resonance, extensive debate, and widespread attention.

The fact that the majority of this Parliament voted in favor of this provision is an important and significant development, as for the first time it did not simply let things slide, but instead took a step forward.
I hope that the Commission and the Council continue along this path.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, I welcome the reports on gender equality and women's poverty in Europe, and I am pleased that this discussion takes place precisely today, as we celebrate the centenary of International Women's Day.

Despite Directive 2002 on equal treatment between men and women in matters of access to employment, training, and promotion, as well as working conditions, further efforts are still required to achieve proper implementation.

Consider that, despite the significant increase in female education in recent years and the fact that the number of female graduates now exceeds that of male graduates, women remain concentrated in traditionally female-dominated sectors—often those with lower pay—and hold fewer leadership positions across all areas of society.

The lack of access to care services for dependent persons, such as children, people with disabilities, and the elderly, along with inadequate parental leave policies and flexible work arrangements, prevents women from working.

I would like to conclude by remembering the women around the world who are fighting for the most fundamental rights, and I would like our Assembly to express solidarity with Iranian feminist associations that are today taking to the streets, calling on women to demonstrate without the hijab.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, to all colleagues who have spoken, I say at last we are once again talking, and I hope we will soon resume taking action, also through targeted choices in economic and common industrial policy.
Thanks to the Lange report, the excellent work carried out by my colleague, and the communication from President Tajani.

This choice means several important things: first, the revival and recovery of the European economy and the sustainability of employment also lie, and perhaps above all, in the recovery and support of everything that is currently underway.
A manufacturing sector, even the most advanced ones as well as the traditional ones, because there a frontier still exists that can be tackled, and this was not so obvious just a short time ago.

Second, the recovery of industrial sectors in Europe means that our competitiveness is no longer determined in the fields of finance and services, but here, and it is not too late to do so—we can seize a challenge for Europe to regain competitiveness worldwide in industrial sectors.

Third point: it has been stated that the new industrial policy focuses on factors rather than sectors, emphasizing factors and tools that can truly foster the emergence of new enterprises and enhance new capacity for resilience and updating.

Therefore, I believe it is important to highlight these new factors requiring action regarding credit, as has already been done. We call for new initiatives, beyond those already announced, including by the Commissioner on the topics of credit, raw materials, and energy costs.

innovation, research, the need to strengthen in Europe the capacity for transferring technological results from research and innovation into enterprises, new products, and new methods and organization of production.

We must recognize the value of a very distinctive fabric within our Europe, particularly in certain countries, namely that of SMEs, the quality of enterprises, the quality of products, in short, the distinctiveness of European industry.
I believe we should rediscover, let's say, these manufacturing traditions, these manufacturing roots, in many countries, in order to renew and transform them.
Finally, the theme of territory, which is very important.
European policies succeed when they intersect with and find a territory capable of addressing them.
Therefore, thanks to Lange, and we look forward to a productive collaboration with the Commission to achieve concrete results in a timely manner.
Many milestones have been achieved, but some battles still lie ahead.
Today, the urgency concerns women from other cultures living in Europe and around the world, in contexts where it is difficult for them to claim minimum standards of dignity and humanity.

We cannot celebrate this festival without placing the issue of women in the Arab Muslim world at the center; otherwise, we continue to ignore a problem that currently affects them, but will soon become ours as well.
We cannot ignore the rising figures of emergency cases that grow year by year, even in Europe, in our cities, in our schools where our children grow up.
For some time, cases of all kinds of violence experienced by Muslim women within their own families have been increasing.

For this reason, I have long been calling for, and I reaffirm today, that Europe implement targeted policies against the spread of Islamic radicalism, forced marriages, honor killings, female genital mutilation, and domestic violence.
We women, European men, must fight for those women who live in Europe, but not only them, who have no voice and are oppressed by the monster of Islamic intellectualism.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, I voted in favor of the resolution on Turkey because the constitutional reform, which took place on September 12, has been unanimously recognized as an important step taken by Turkey towards Europe.
Naturally, many steps still remain to be taken, and in this regard I express concern over the numerous arrests of journalists.

It should be noted that Turkey is the seventh largest trading partner of the European Union, and that the European Union is Turkey's primary trading partner; moreover, trade exchanges, properly interpreted over the centuries, have fostered mutual penetration and peaceful understanding among peoples.

I therefore commend the significant progress made in advancing connections between the European Union and Turkey, particularly those via the maritime highways crossing the northern Adriatic and the northern Tyrrhenian.
On which a flow exceeding 250 t RANUY now moves in both directions, connected to a major railway and trucking link from port to internal European destinations.
A significant amount of truck traffic on roads has been eliminated, and I hope this progress continues along those lines.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, with today's vote we have given further momentum toward a definitive solution to this issue, but we have not voted for the best possible solution.

Indeed, I would like to recall that the more than 300 amendments submitted in the report have been transformed into 38 compromise amendments, all adopted, aimed at better defining the priority areas of the strategy, that is, the objectives of this strategy.

The Commission, through the permanent task force, shall ensure the collection and dissemination of statistical data and best practices, and Member States should appoint a government official, hopefully of high level, serving as a contact point for the implementation of the strategy.
Yet we could have been even more effective and less hypocritical if, in the report, we had emphasized the obligations and duties that the Roma community should nonetheless respect in host countries.
This is my hope, namely that solutions will soon be found that enable better coexistence of this community across all member states.
Lampedusa has been overwhelmed by tens of thousands of North Africans, an island that could never, under any circumstances, accommodate such numbers.
Italy has been under attack for weeks by hundreds of clandestine boats.
If we do not all react together, if the EU does not react, the wave of people fleeing will continue.
The undocumented immigrants who arrived in recent months must be sent home; there is no alternative.
But has the Italian government, beyond a few words of solidarity, been left alone to manage this biblical exodus of unforeseeable proportions—where is Europe?
To date, Brussels has been complacently inactive, adopting a shameful stance that my fellow citizens can no longer tolerate.
Europe has left Italy alone to face an unprecedented emergency.

Instead, Europe not only must assist Italy in the repatriation of undocumented migrants, but must also support the Italian government's efforts to prevent thousands upon thousands of people from continuing to depart, particularly from Tunisia.

All of this, however, applies to Libyan refugees, towards whom Europe must stand alongside Italy in hydration and logistical management operations, but above all must commit to ensuring that refugees are hosted across all member states.
The reception of those fleeing war cannot fall solely on Italy.
It is time to stop all this chatter.
The European Union, if it exists, should make its presence felt.
We cannot tolerate Italy being filled with undocumented immigrants, while France rejects them using shameful methods.
Illegal immigrants must be sent back to their home countries, while all member states must commit now to hosting Libyan refugees.
The issue of ensuring adequate financial resources to guarantee the decommissioning processes of nuclear plants is one already addressed by the European Commission.

European Union legislation on nuclear sector safety is currently of great relevance, given of course the recent international events and the resulting decisions taken by the Union's governments on this matter.

The work of the honourable Marinescu, which we have expressed our support for, addresses the specific cases of certain countries that, at the time of their accession, undertook particular obligations regarding the decommissioning of nuclear plants and which received specific support from the European Union precisely for this activity.
Beyond specific cases, an effective verification and inspection mechanism must be established for nuclear power plants located within the territory of the Union.
Indeed, a high number of reactors are estimated to be shut down in the near future within the community territory.

It will therefore be essential to ensure guarantee mechanisms, also and especially in these cases, for the allocation of adequate financial resources necessary to meet both ordinary and extraordinary needs.
I would also like to thank the rapporteur for his report.
The report addresses the developments that have occurred in the changes to control and communication systems.
For this reason, a picture of significant interregional disparity emerges, particularly among states, in the reporting of irregularities and frauds.

The report repeatedly highlights the peculiarity of the low levels of fraud and irregularities reported by various countries in relation to agricultural expenditures, structural funds, and pre-accession funds.
Indeed, here we must clarify the statistical data that have been provided.
The situation is further exacerbated by the well-known ineffectiveness of recoveries.
However, one point is clearly apparent and confirms a long-term trend.

The fight for legality and transparency in the expenditure of European resources, as well as the struggle against the evasion and avoidance of revenues, cannot yet rely on full cooperation from the states, governments, and European institutions.

In this sense, I consider timely the calls for revising the Community legislation on public procurement and the need to establish what I refer to as a European public registry of beneficiaries of Community funds.
I thank the rapporteurs and also Commissioner Barnier for his great willingness to cooperate with the Parliament.
We advocate a conception of the internal market capable of encompassing and meeting social expectations and the social dimensions they entail.
It is not a utopia, but realism and also modernity.

It is precisely this perspective that makes the market itself stronger, because the sole economic element—economicism, in essence—the reduction of the market to economicism—has demonstrated that this approach fails to resolve either the problems of growth or those of cohesion.
In short, it means fully realizing, in the present, the entirely European concept of a social and market economy.
In today's texts, this is present.
There is recognition of social enterprises, cooperatives, their role, mutuals, foundations—the 10% of European enterprises and a large part of our employment.
They are realities that are genuine businesses, demonstrating economic pluralism and pluralism among enterprises.

They work with capital but not for capital, and they aim to achieve labor, welfare, quality, and social innovation, demonstrating that these values must and can be part of the internal market in Europe.

Mr. President, Beijing's justification that the artist Huey Huey was arrested for economic crimes is unconvincing, as is the notion that such intense pressure is being exerted against a free and dissident artist.
It is unconvincing, as this arrest takes place within hundreds of arrests of opponents and the systematic repression of any dissent.

On the days when the Arab uprisings erupt, our thoughts go to the image of cruel violence in Tien Amen Square against young Egyptians who, as today's revolutionaries of the Jasmine Revolution demand, were calling for freedom and democracy.
China has opened up to the West economically, yet has failed to integrate production with environmental respect and workplace safety, and shows no opening whatsoever toward democracy and pluralism.

Our stance is not an interference in China's internal affairs, as Beijing claims, but rather an appeal from men and women, from movements, from political parties committed to building a world free from intolerance on every continent.

The government in Beijing should observe what is happening in the world to understand that if it does not change, it will be historical events and the Chinese citizens themselves who will bring about change in China.
Jobs lost despite several million euros invested in support policies.

This is the disappointing outcome in terms of impact on SMEs, of the Lisbon Agenda I project and the copycat Europe 2020 strategy, on which it is even being considered to concentrate the resources of the upcoming multiannual financial framework 2014.

I can therefore only reiterate what thousands of European citizens and taxpayers are asking: what purpose does Europe serve if it is unable to resolve the continent's economic problems, cannot speak with one voice on foreign policy, and squanders money?
I hope that what I have repeatedly stated since Commissioner Tajani will come to pass.
I hope the revision of the SMEs Code is truly capable of simplifying bureaucracy, ensuring access to and recovery of credit, and facilitating interbanking.
With the directive on late payment, a concrete and tangible achievement has been reached, yet one stemming from political will rather than europropaganda.

If the costs of yet another strategy will still fund nothing but empty talk, I urge European institutions to make amends toward the dozens of small entrepreneurs in my region, Veneto, who took their own lives before witnessing the collapse of their small businesses.

Mr. President, while Europe pretends not to notice, the 2011 Europol report on organized crime states that with the accession of Romania and Bulgaria to Schengen, powerful Turkish, Russian, and Albanian criminal organizations will be able to expand their activities throughout Europe.
Moreover, I quote literally: illegal immigration from Turkey will extend to the coasts of Bulgaria, and drug and human trafficking will increase in the hands of Turkish-Albanian crime.

Regarding human trafficking, Romania and Bulgaria's accession to Schengen could provide Bulgarian crime groups and the new Roma communities with new opportunities to expand their already substantial trafficking activities across the rest of Europe.
In short, increased illegal immigration from Turkey and expanded trafficking in drugs and human beings result in reduced security for European citizens.
Europe wants to do a great favor to organized crime.
The Northern League party is and will always be opposed to this kind of reckless decision.
Anyone voting in favor of this report tomorrow will bear responsibility for a decision that will open a highway for organized crime straight to the heart of Europe.
Thanks to the Treaty of Amsterdam, environmental policy and sustainability policy have gained greater importance, and the report has been well received.
Attention has focused on the integration between economic and environmental policy, and on incorporating environmental issues into policies and other sectors.

Among the key areas are climate change, sustainable transport, nature, biodiversity, health and the environment, as well as the use of natural resources and waste management, and the international dimension of sustainable development.
In June 2006, the European Council, in its conclusions, invited the Union and its Member States to extend national accounts to encompass the key aspects of sustainable development.
National accounts must therefore be supplemented by an integrated economic and environmental accounting system, capable of providing fully coherent data.
I voted in favor of this regulatory proposal because it establishes new uses for national accounting data, environmental statistics, and statistics in other sectors.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, my vote in favor of the Garriga report on the Union's upcoming financial perspectives stems from the conviction that it is precisely during one of the most severe crises that progress can be achieved.
that the so-called European spirit be decisive in emphasizing and achieving the necessity of providing credible tools to the Union, starting from an adequate financial endowment.
Therefore, the request for an additional 5% in the next budget is a just and necessary request, perhaps not sufficient, but essential to provide answers to the major challenges before us.
It is necessary to address real issues, such as stronger cohesion among regions, greater research and innovation capacity, and a stronger ability to generate growth and provide employment.

If conditions are adequate, none of these issues will find a sufficient resolution, starting with the introduction of this intermediate category into cohesion policy, which only makes sense if it does not cause harm to the other existing categories.
This is why I voted in favor, and I hope the Commission and the Council will follow the guidance provided by this report.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the new Hungarian Constitution reduces the ability of oversight bodies, such as the Constitutional Court, to intervene and increases the powers of the executive, thereby undermining the normal balance of powers.
Social rights are being emptied out; the state provides subsidies to the needy only if they agree to perform public service work.
The right to self-defense is finally established, allowing individuals to keep weapons at home without a license.
The right to abortion is prohibited, and strongly discriminatory rules are introduced against homosexuals.
All of this is contrary to Directive No. 78, which establishes a general framework for combating discrimination.

I therefore ask the Commissioner how she intends to act, given that the fundamental rights established by the new Hungarian Constitution are poorly compatible with those of the European Union and the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, we are about to vote on a strategic document of great importance, which provides a solid framework for the negotiations on the financial perspectives beyond 2013.

starting from the requirement of coherence between objectives and instruments, between the new role that European institutions are called upon to play after Lisbon and in the face of recession, and the resources we collectively decide to invest.
This coherence also implies a serious response to the demand for innovation emerging from public opinion, EU policy beneficiaries, and European social and productive forces.
We did so for cohesion, a crucial chapter in determining what we want and what the European Union should be in the coming years.
It is said that cohesion today is a vital cornerstone of the European project, an important heritage for all to safeguard, protect, and renew.
Particularly critical on this point is the effort made to introduce an intermediate category of regions, especially since in many cases the years of recession we have experienced in the past have

We have reduced internal disparities, making it even more crucial to provide efficient and adequate support for Europe's less developed regions, by combating waste and promoting a clear development policy.
Yet I believe that the challenge posed by intermediate categories with the safeguard clause is one we must accept in order to usher in a new phase in European policy.
of cohesion in Europe, and also to give substance to the cohesion policy with the more balanced, more sustainable development that we aim to ensure for Europe in the coming years.
I too join in thanking the honourable Langen and the other colleagues with whom we have worked to produce this complex report.
I believe it is particularly important what the honourable von der Leyen has recalled.
Langen, when you said that we wish to maintain the scope, the field of application of this regulation, in keeping with the mandate given by the G20 at the 2009 Pittsburgh summit.
The fundamental objective is to bring the majority of over-the-counter derivatives onto regulated platforms in order to avoid continuing to be exposed to a high degree of
When one reaches the point— as has occurred— of exchanging and guaranteeing 400 million dollars without collateral, that is, without adequate reserves, one understands what risks we are running.

Having said that, I would also like to briefly mention two issues: the first is the relationship with the Council, and the second is the coherence of the proposal for the reform of financial markets that we must advance.

I raise the first issue regarding the Council, and I say this very clearly: the feeling is all too often that the Council acts as an obstacle to swiftly and effectively advancing financial markets reform.
I hope the new Presidency will take up this issue, because once again we must take this into account
On account of the positions, difficulties, and conflicts that emerge during Council proceedings, the delay is largely attributable to these factors.
It is an issue that also affects the procedure we wish to follow.
The second issue concerns the coherence and specificity of the measures.
There is not only the measure concerning OTC, but also the measure on short selling and others.

I would like to highlight two issues: the specific matter of the raw materials market, a particular market requiring special anti-speculative measures, and the issue of local authorities' exposure to derivatives in European Union countries.
I believe these are issues on which the Parliament must act.
Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the Fukushima incident continues to produce very serious consequences, with the release of radioactive seawater and water exceeding legal limits by six times.
The risk that food products become contaminated is high.
Europe has established very strict controls, but these apply exclusively to fresh products.
No protective regulation has been issued for processed products, in particular for canned fish.
We import 370 tonnes of canned tuna, and the main suppliers are countries in Southeast Asia, which import fish from Japan.
This means that canned fish imported into Europe might contain Japanese fish.
It is essential and urgent to provide accurate information regarding the place of origin.
Today, consumers know where the box was produced, but not where the fish was caught.

The Commission has recently acknowledged this issue and intends to address the gap through the reform of the common fisheries policy, which will not enter into force before two years, just as the Sommer report under discussion in this plenary session will not become effective before three years.
We must not create alarmism, but consumers have the right to urgent and transparent information.
The Commission must act as soon as possible.
Mr. President Tusk, your speech gives us good hope; the hope is that the Polish Presidency will be a contribution to the fight against an Europe of selfishness.
that it may be a contribution in favor of an Europe that truly sees itself as an integrated reality with a shared destiny.
But as you rightly stated in your intervention, a presidency faces concrete problems to address and resolve.
In this brief time, I would like to highlight two crucial issues regarding the economic and financial crisis.
First issue: the reform of financial markets.
It is not a technical issue; it is a major democratic question of our time. The timelines are far too long; we must resolve matters such as the reform of short selling and OTC derivatives.
Second point: we face the issue of taxing financial transactions; we must address it substantively, and we expect a concrete contribution from the Polish Presidency in this regard.
More than twenty years have passed since the signing of the Schengen Agreement.
No one doubts the benefits of free circulation, but we must acknowledge that the current framework is no longer suitable for present and future needs.

For this reason, Member States and the EU as a whole must commit to reviewing the operational mechanisms of Schengen, so that freedom of movement does not come at the expense of our citizens' legitimate need for security.

To the extent of our current knowledge, the Commission's guidelines may also be partially acceptable, particularly regarding the introduction of a mechanism allowing Member States to temporarily assume border controls in case of emergency.
One must, however, consider how this will occur and whether actions will follow the statements, and above all, tangible results.

A comprehensive reform of the European border protection system will undoubtedly also be required, particularly regarding external borders, on which Frontex must become as soon as possible an agency with genuine operational and supportive powers for national authorities.
We must protect our external borders if we wish to continue with Schengen.
It is time for bold choices in this Europe, if one does not wish to merely survive.

The crisis has profoundly affected the everyday lives of European citizens and businesses, and risks, if we fail to respond, lowering irreversibly the social and cultural quality, well-being, and future prospects of young people and Europeans.

Exiting the crisis means creating remedies for the identified causes—remedies against speculation, which may require even cutting off its claws if necessary, and imposing strict rules on financial markets, remedies that cannot be called into question.

Remedies against recession, remedies against an unbalanced model of development that has placed profit above the person, remedies against the resurgent nationalism that prevents Europe from making the choices that are rightfully its own.

Above all, it is time to define the tools, resources—even new ones—to move forward, to ignite the engines of growth through research, innovation, training, and support for productive investments, infrastructure, and employment, because without work people lack dignity and rights.
This report, thanks to Pervaiz Berès and his colleagues, outlines a possible strategy for a proud Europe that is aware of its role.
to European leadership, if any exists, the responsibility now falls to implement it.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, behind the widespread prosperity of our continent lies a hidden Europe, often invisible, which we discuss in terms of numbers, statistics, and sociology rather than as real people—often women and children or the elderly, that is, the most vulnerable population.
But Europe will not be credible to its citizens unless it learns to be consistent, and we here wish to be consistent in this Parliament, at this stage in Europe's life.

And so, if we claim to base the Union on values, on human rights, I don't think we can so drastically cut or terminate a program for those who do not eat, in short, for those who lack the right to food, to access to food.

The key elements for an unresolved legal issue are the agricultural fund, the social fund, and, if it is to be social, Madam Commissioner, it must not come at the expense of other social programs, but rather be a new, dedicated fund with appropriate resources.
We claim to want to ensure food security for Europeans, but the first right to security is the right to have food, which should then also be healthy.
Then here is our consistency and our commitment.

We must therefore seek a legal solution that ensures continuity and, with the support of non-governmental organizations and volunteer groups, guarantees the fundamental human right to civic responsibility.

review of the common fisheries policy and the underlying option, which is the regionalization of a policy that in recent years, having been centralized, has demonstrated all its weaknesses.

I believe the elements outlined in colleague Iotova's report describe a particular situation, yet one that is replicable across the rest of Europe. The situation in the Adriatic Sea within the Mediterranean region is no different.
in the Tyrrhenian or in the geo sea, that is, the interactions—especially between European fishing and extra-European fishing—are often highly damaging to European markets and European consumers.
The second issue I would like to briefly highlight is the reference to a common coast guard.
There is no politics without the exercise of oversight.

Today, controls across Europe are carried out by disparate forces; I believe the time has come to consider how one of the first pillars, so to speak, of European order—the coast guards—could achieve common management, greater coordination, and why not, a unified European body.
With this hope, I fully support the contents of my colleague Iotova's report.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the disaster that struck the Gulf of Mexico more than a year ago has prompted the European Commission to reconsider legislation relating to the activities of short-range oil platforms.
The European Parliament, with this report, has also wished to express its position on the matter, adopting a text that requires appropriate technical and financial capabilities.
aimed at sector operators and designed to establish high safety standards, thereby reinforcing the European Union's leading role in promoting the safety of offshore activities.
The new interventions requested of the Commission must focus on international coordination and a realistic approach that combines all existing strengths
in the sector at the level of procedures and avoid the creation of new bodies or additional bureaucracy, which could risk diverting attention from achieving concrete final objectives.

The report, in concrete terms, calls not only for greater accountability on the part of operating companies but also for improved mechanisms of communication and exchange of information and knowledge, and expresses the hope for a strengthening and expansion of the role of the European Maritime Safety Agency's expertise.
For all these reasons, I consider the outcome achieved—thanks to the negotiation on this text, which we have supported—to be positive.
I thank my colleagues Seeber and Schillaciakis, and I also thank Commissioner Heidegger.

I believe we are gathered here today to discuss and deliberate with the genuine aim of achieving a robust European climate policy, capable of safeguarding economic development and the well-being of European citizens.
For this reason, Mr. President, international climate policies, as has been recalled so far, have primarily focused on reducing CO2 emissions.
This proposal highlights how we must also address the fight against other anthropogenic emissions that contribute to the formation of tropospheric ozone, thereby impacting climate change.

Many things have already been mentioned, which I believe it is important to keep clearly in mind: today we are capable of doing so at absolutely reasonable costs, we have the technologies available, and, regardless of any uncertainties, it is CO2 emissions that primarily contribute.

Although it is uncertain whether the other greenhouse gases present in the atmosphere are present in much smaller percentages, it is uncertain that their potential is very high, and therefore we may truly achieve significant results within just a few years, within just a few decades.
They are two important ones.

There are signals; as the Commissioner also reminded us in the United States, I have seen a study published in Nature by the scientific agency of the American Department of Commerce, which leads me to believe that it is possible

There are many points of convergence with regard to the analysis carried out by our colleagues, and thus I imagine these will also be factors enabling Europe to present itself in Durban with a position that is even stronger, more concrete, and more significant.

I conclude only by recalling, as has already been recalled, that during this exceptionally difficult and delicate moment we are experiencing—discussing the homeless just a few minutes ago—there is a sensitivity among European citizens that we must take into account; therefore, considering that the great

Regarding sensitivity, obviously the top priority—and for jobs—we should be able to carry out a communication campaign that clearly explains to citizens what we concretely aim to achieve in terms of development, their well-being, and the protection of jobs.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the single market, free movement, and the Treaty of Lisbon are all elements that have come into effect regardless of any change in our manner of legislation, in the European way, or in the national way.

If follow-up is required on the commitments arising from these opportunities, it is particularly necessary to genuinely initiate a phase of simplification of the legislative system, a phase of simplification that must generate a different dialogue between Europe and the individual states.

I take the opportunity to suggest a kind of clarity test for our documents when we produce a white paper or a green paper; everything must be made significantly more comprehensible, and I would even say that the true, perhaps strategic, proposal for our Europe is to actually launch

The solution to this, let's say virtuous, initial phase of registration is to assign a specific mandate to a European Commissioner whose sole responsibility would be to clarify legislation and the relationships between the Member States and Europe itself.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, Mr. Representative of the Council, and Madam Commissioner, I too would like, as other colleagues have already done, to positively welcome the anti-corruption package approved by the European Commission on 6 June.
Corruption is a transnational phenomenon, and combating it requires measures on a European scale.
The vast majority of our European citizens, according to Eurobarometer estimates, believe that Europe should do more.
How could one blame them?
In many Member States, there has been a lack of political will to combat corruption; indeed, there are still European countries that have not signed the United Nations Convention against Corruption.

When considering the devastating impact of corruption on democratic processes, citizens' rights, and trust in the rule of law, it is evident that the national anti-corruption measures adopted so far by Member States are insufficient to contain a phenomenon that is on the rise.

It is the European Union that must provide a unified and robust response, especially now, amid an unprecedented economic crisis weighing heavily on people's lives, the sustainability of pension and welfare systems, and the capacity of institutions to ensure essential services.
Corruption causes an annual loss of 120 billion euros, equivalent to 1% of the European Union's GDP, and daily diverts resources from the Union and its member states.
They could be used to provide essential services—education, childcare, employment, transportation—thereby diverting clean resources away from supporting people.

Therefore, greater courage and firmness are required, both in monitoring the implementation of anti-corruption measures and in intensifying and strengthening anti-corruption measures at the level of the Union.

In particular, we cannot speak of fighting corruption if we are unable to adopt at the very least minimum standards for defining the crime of corruption, which are entirely absent from the recently adopted package.
The same applies to the definition of uniform parameters for measuring the extent of the phenomenon across member countries, which is essential for preparing an effective response.
We support, for example, revitalizing the EU network of contact points against corruption, as proposed in the resolution we will vote on tomorrow.
The crisis is not of yesterday; it has already existed for several years.
Perhaps it is now time to conduct a critical review of the policies pursued so far. Was everything always done correctly?
We have always carried out appropriate analyses of the situation.
Was the right approach chosen?
The same European Central Bank, which—as Minister Rostovsky stated—has saved Europe, has always acted appropriately, even when, fearing inflation, it raised interest rates?
President Barroso identifies a problem: the speed of markets and the slowness of politics, but this is the only problem.

Certainly, if one considers that we are still in the implementation phase of 21 July, this should at least suggest the need to better prepare and organize summits and their outcomes; however, politics will always be slower than markets.
The point, then, is to look further ahead, shifting the emphasis to the fight against unemployment and development.

This is the ground that the European Commission must choose today, as evidenced in President Barroso's speech: the more concrete the discussion on the deficit, the less attention there was to growth and development guidelines.

The Commission must overcome its weakness and state clearly that the priority must be combating unemployment and creating jobs, redistributing wealth, and implementing a public investment strategy, while also revising the rules of the new European Stability Pact.
Mr. President, I believe the challenge of the coming years is to ensure that Europe, as has been said, maintains its position as the leading global power.
a global tourist destination and, at the same time, be able to enhance, in terms of environmental preservation as well as competitive capacity, its entire territorial heritage.

It is a challenging and by no means guaranteed endeavor, especially considering not only the opportunities but also the aggressiveness of other countries in this sector, from other continents, ranging from China to India to Brazil.
Now, in this context, as Commissioner Tajani recalled, it becomes essential, as also highlighted in the opinion of the Commission that we adopted in the Committee on Regional Development.
to combine a strong overall strategy for tourism across the European Union with an appreciation of the role of regions and territories.
In this sense, full involvement from the outset is essential, in respect of the principle of subsidiarity of regions and local authorities.
Moreover, I believe that the development of integrated tourism networks and projects should be encouraged, starting with the establishment of partnerships for the exchange of best practices.
It seems appropriate, too, to propose establishing a Europe brand capable of defining high quality in the tourist product.
Obviously, it will be up to the Commission to further define the priorities, particularly from a financial standpoint, to ensure that Europe's leadership is maintained in the future.

Madam President, honourable colleagues, the preparation of the common strategic framework for funding research and innovation represents one of the greatest opportunities that European development and economy have before them.

It is indeed about acting on the lever that can truly trigger a moment of growth and also of new qualified employment, such as that for researchers and young people who dedicate themselves precisely to the new opportunities in innovation, technology, and related professions.
One observation, above all, emerges from this work, and that is the need for us to increasingly emphasize in Europe research and innovation that lead to practical application, that reach

We must foster the creation of new enterprises that lead to new patents and do not remain merely basic research or pure knowledge—important indeed, but not sufficient to truly trigger the acceleration of development.

The second observation concerns the relationship with local authorities, regions, and territories, which also represent an important instrument for territorial programming and for fostering collaboration among research initiatives, universities, centers of excellence, and enterprises.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, European schools were established with the aim of ensuring access to education in one's mother tongue for the children of European Union officials called upon to work and live abroad in a cultural environment different from that of their country of origin.
The first European school was established in Luxembourg in 1953, initiated by a group of officials.
Over the years, considering the positive outcomes of this form of teaching, a total of 14 European Schools have been established within the various Member States.

Although acknowledging that the system of European schools represents a necessity rather than a luxury, it is evident that more than 50 years after the establishment of the first European school, this system may exhibit outdated features and must in some way be updated and adapted to new economic and social demands.

I therefore expressed my favourable vote on the text submitted by colleague Cavada, which aims at greater involvement by the European Union to support and coordinate the actions of Member States tasked with developing and improving the system of European schools.
It is a venture that involves investing; we know that investing in education means investing in the future of our European youth.

Mr. President, honorable colleagues, in recent decades, there has been an exponential increase in the number of natural disasters causing the deaths of tens of thousands of people across various parts of the world.
Last year, within the Committee on Development, I followed the work on the report concerning the European Union's capacity to respond to disasters.
How much remained to be done to ensure a coordinated response from the European Union in the face of the growing number of earthquakes, floods, or tsunamis?
The European Union's response to the Haiti earthquake, for example, clearly demonstrated the complexity of the existing tools and procedures.

Although Member States and Community institutions acted promptly in providing humanitarian aid, the crisis management has raised a series of issues concerning efficiency, coordination, and transparency.
Precisely for this reason, I voted in favor of the text submitted by my colleague Elisabetta Gardini, which aims at greater efficiency in all phases of disaster management, and greater coordination.
among the involved parties and through simplification aimed at avoiding unnecessary overlaps and time losses that could hinder the timely and effective delivery of assistance.

I believe the time has come for Europe, precisely in order to safeguard this important sector, to launch an initiative in which the conception of the sea and the conception of the coasts is not limited to a kind of urban planning that ends at the shoreline and fails to encompass the sea as a whole.
I believe an integrated policy in this regard is useful, just as we must develop everything underlying the claims made about rural tourism.
an excellent source of income for disadvantaged areas, for regions where employment no longer exists, where it is crucial not to continue with depopulation and instead ensure human settlement.

Promoting tourism in rural areas could mean paying lower taxes. Perhaps only in this way would a genuine opportunity truly be created for both the tourism resource and those who live there.
