The same idea, translating it into actions at the level of institutions, allocation of resources, funding, and coordination of measures among the various levels of government within the Member States of the Union.

An approach worthy of appreciation and reward, given the sector in question, with its implications in terms of GDP contribution, employment, and new opportunities for small and medium-sized enterprises.
enterprises, attention to the protection and enhancement of the environment becomes capable of developing and thus of addressing the main challenges posed by the current situation.
For all these reasons, I consider this report positive, an important signal in the direction of promoting a European policy for tourism.
Mr. President, even for recovery, three small considerations: we need more Europe here as well.
I believe it is inevitable that on certain matters there is indeed an approach in which more Europe is not only desired, but actively built.
More Europe means, for example, genuinely beginning to consider a common road police force and identical rules regarding the protection of safety.
More Europe means having a European training policy, rather than simply delegating this to individual states, but instead building, for example, a genuine training policy.
towards schools, towards young people, and also towards categories that are somewhat higher risk—just mentioned a moment ago, motorcyclists, but truck drivers could also be mentioned.
For us, more Europe means having common tools within machines that improve safety conditions, anti-alcohol and anti-corruption measures.
Drowsiness—I believe these should be targeted strategies developed in collaboration with automobile manufacturers and European vehicle production.
I believe that if all this falls within the framework of a shared political commitment, I truly think that perhaps the conditions will improve.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, this report addresses a topic that is global food security. "Global" means that this issue is not merely about world hunger in the poorest countries, but that food supply concerns affect all communities, including those in the most developed nations.

Sketches some key lines, as essential, of the coherence between European policies and this goal of global food security, because we are an institution that preaches very well but is often not consistent in its choices.
I only wish to outline, however, three additional innovative themes that I believe we should develop further.
The first risk to food supply today stems from price volatility caused by speculation on food products, derivatives, etc., which must be contained, if not prevented.
Second, the issue of land grabbing, which I believe must be the subject of some form of treaty or negotiation, as democracy itself is at stake, along with the survival of many peoples.
Third, the coherence between supply and energy policies.
We cannot, in order to produce new energy, prevent food from being produced for the world's peoples.
I voted firmly in favor of the honorable Zimmer's report, primarily for reasons of safety, as this is an idea that has not yet been accepted.
It is purely political in nature, because I believe it is necessary to bring the major issue of food security back to the heart of the European Union's overall initiative.
It is not merely a matter of helping developing countries address this issue more effectively, but also due to the food crisis, as food demand is expected to increase by more than 70 percent by 2050.

Agricultural products are a major issue, which, if not addressed in a timely and decisive manner, will become one of the primary threats—perhaps the most significant threat—to stability, peace, and even democracy, as has already been demonstrated.

For this reason, I believe it is essential that the entire European Union, together with its member states, make every necessary effort—starting with adequate economic investments, as highlighted in the report—in this direction, in order to seriously address this major issue.

Hello to the Commission, I must truly thank all the individuals who have contributed over these weeks, these months, to the production of this document which we are voting on today in plenary session.
We worked, I must say, very effectively, and a truly constructive debate emerged.
The resolution proposal we are voting on today therefore reflects the outcome of truly collaborative work and is the result of the contributions of all political forces and the entire Parliament.

I believe a shared and very strong sensitivity has developed regarding this issue, precisely because we must remember that we live in an era truly characterized by an exponential increase in disasters.
The figures, if we look at them in their raw form, are very telling—the number of disasters worldwide has increased fivefold, considering that in 1975
Seventy-eight catastrophic events had been recorded, whereas today we are experiencing nearly 400 disastrous events per year.

Mr. President, unfortunately, in the past twenty years the victims of these disasters have amounted to 90, and over 29 million people have been affected by the disasters, while economic losses total 211 billion euros.
It is clear that we are facing phenomena of such magnitude that demand a stronger response, a more effective response, and a truly strong commitment from us.
We all have in our minds the images of the earthquake in Haiti, the floods in Pakistan, and what occurred in Japan.
Mr. President, Europe itself witnesses sudden floods and wildfires each year on its territory, and let us recall the ash cloud following the eruption of the Icelandic volcano.
There is no need to go further to affirm how important the work being carried out here in Parliament is, guided by two principles.
fundamental ones, which are our guiding stars—the principle of solidarity, reinforced by the solidarity clause in the Treaty of Lisbon, and the principle of subsidiarity.
We move along these lines.

Mr. President, the European Union's intervention has always been generous and effective in the past, as I believe is demonstrated by the diligent work of our Commissioner Georgieva; but today we need to take a qualitative leap.
A leap in quality that we are ready to make, shifting from an ad hoc response to one where the reaction
enhanced through the proactive identification of existing resources available in Member States and made accessible to the European Civil Protection Mechanism.
It is also important to emphasize this qualitative leap, moving from sharing information and responding to emergencies to assuming the role of planning and monitoring.

This is the coordination we hope for, the crucial role of services such as GMES, and I take this opportunity to also express the concern regarding what is happening with the budget, and with these essential new technologies.
We have emphasized the importance of communication with citizens, as the Japanese experience has taught us this as well, and it is never sufficient, never enough.
And we await from the Commission, to which we ask to present as soon as possible the legislative proposal regarding civil protection.
Ethnic minorities oppressed, Christians forced to secretly live their faith, priests brutally killed while authorities remain silent—this is today's Turkey.
My party, the Northern League, has always been opposed to Turkey's entry into Europe, as it represents a potential Trojan horse for radical Islamism on our continent.
Today more than ever, we are convinced that the EU must put an end to the accession negotiations.
The intolerable threats that Turkey has directed against Europe must receive an immediate response.
One cannot accept that a third country dictates to Europe what it must do.
Cyprus is a full member state of the EU and has an indisputable right to assume the rotating presidency.
Europe must send a clear signal and demonstrate its readiness to defend Cyprus with every available means against Turkey's political and military arrogance and aggression.
We immediately suspend the accession negotiations and remain prepared to also suspend commercial relations.
Mr. President, we are experiencing a decisive turning point for the future of the euro and, with it, for the entire European project.
Having this awareness would mean moving beyond a myopic logic that subordinates everything to an ideological imperative of budgetary balance.
We all want the accounts of various states to be in order, we all want to reduce indebtedness.
However, it is clear that without a sound investment policy capable of revitalizing the economy, there will be no possibility of growth.
The only clear outcome in this setup is that heavier prices, as always, will be borne by the most vulnerable groups.
For this reason, together with the group of Socialists and Democrats, we voted against this report.
Indeed, one continues in a single direction, that of rigor without growth, a direction that has proven incorrect thus far.
I greatly appreciate the work that colleague Ferrera has done with this report.
Indeed, among the package of six legislative proposals concerning economic governance, only on this one did I and my group cast a positive vote.

We did so because, among other things, a clause protecting social rights through collective bargaining has been introduced, and furthermore, the role of Parliament in adopting delegated acts has been strengthened.

Therefore, although we cannot alter the overall balance of the package of six proposals, this report certainly represents a step forward, among other things, toward a surveillance system that takes fundamental rights into account.

And thus, in this context, I maintain that, in our view, a perspective based solely on an austerity policy does not yield positive results; this report certainly constitutes a positive note in contradiction.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, Finland and the Netherlands have raised this issue in Council for a very simple reason: Bulgaria and Romania are currently unable to ensure effective border control, and there are three instances of serious corruption involving the political class and organized crime.
Thinking about it this way, it is not the political parties and populist movements, but Europol, in its 2010 report on organized crime.
There is no populist propaganda, but a serious security issue.

Should Bulgaria enter the Schengen area at this moment, police and security agencies foresee that Bulgarian borders—particularly the maritime ones—would become an easy target for Turkish and Eastern European crime, and Bulgaria and Romania would become the crossroads for human trafficking and drug smuggling.

It is natural that some Member States have significant reservations about Bulgaria and Romania's actual ability to effectively control their borders against organized crime and corruption, and it is therefore entirely natural that they have opposed their accession to Schengen.
Allowing this now would pave the way for transnational crime and create a breach in European borders, precisely where there is intense migratory pressure from Turkey.
The reasons for opposing it are many, and Europe cannot ignore them.
I therefore wish to know the intentions of the Commission and the Council regarding this delicate matter, which in my opinion has so far been handled with
insufficiency and without taking into account the risks associated with the accession of Bulgaria and Romania, according to competent bodies specializing in security and international crime.

In my view, the Commission and the Council should postpone the accession of these two countries to Schengen and grant approval only when there is reasonable assurance that the accession of Bulgaria and Romania will not affect the security of European citizens.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the crisis in Europe is intertwining the problems of public finances and private capital with those of our enterprises' productivity, and the spectre of recession is real.
If therefore the priority given to SMEs is crucial, I ask the Commission that, together with Parliament, these principles—our sound principles—must absolutely be put into practice.
in practice, that the legislative initiatives of the SBA, the Small Business Act, become concrete practice among the Member States, with high levels of effectiveness and quality.

Consider, Mr. Commissioner, what would not be achieved if all Member States accelerated the implementation of the directive on payment delays? What a significant advantage it would be for SMEs! Thus, SME evidence is important, but we are still at the methodological level.

It is certainly right to verify whether, when we legislate, we are truly doing so in the interest of and with due consideration for small and medium-sized enterprises; however, we ask that progress be made with great consistency and determination, indeed with depth, in building the framework of opportunities that You have outlined.
We appreciate the efforts being made also by the Commission, and we are ready—with the budget and other resources at our disposal—to support it.
I will say only one word about credit.
Let's have a clear conversation with the banks, I say this to you, Commissioner, because we must support small and medium-sized enterprises.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, I have expressed my support for the text submitted by my colleague, Member Wallis.

Without prejudicing the usual avenues of access to justice, I am convinced that an alternative dispute resolution (ADR) mechanism represents a swift and cost-effective alternative to legal litigation.
In addition to being an effective tool for small and medium-sized enterprises, ADR can also see significant development online for the resolution of minor disputes.

Therefore, through joint action with the Member States, it is necessary to foster the creation of a harmonized legal framework that, by strengthening citizens' trust in the internal market, can contribute to stimulating the European economy.

The report concerning the provisions for sectors placed on the market under the flexibility regime, which we have supported with our favorable vote, has been guided by a measured and realistic approach, resulting in a comprehensive report

on one hand, by avoiding setting unrealistic and unattainable requirements, and on the other hand, by establishing a so-called flexibility regime that includes defined time periods and corresponding quantitative work limits.

permitted emissions, entails the annual release onto the market of a pre-established quota of tractors meeting specific characteristics, thus allowing gradual compliance with set standards over time, without causing disruptions to the affected production and industrial sectors.

Mr. Tartarella, the work accomplished has been significant, and you have skillfully achieved a careful balance between the demands posed by the major challenges the European Union is called upon to address at this time and those related to environmental protection and the current economic crisis.
Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the current global economic crisis, even more severe than that of 1929, has caused unsustainable imbalances in international economies over the past years.
Commercial imbalances among countries, financial markets lacking common rules, and instability in banking systems are all potential threats to financial and economic stability.
This resolution, which I express my support for, urgently underscores the need for comprehensive reform, new rules, and global economic coordination.

The economy requires a global governance system, open markets, high economic and social standards, more responsible monetary policy, and stricter supervisory policies, with decision-making bodies and international economic institutions serving as the principal actors at the heart of this new approach.

Europe, in particular, needs a transparent framework to reduce excessive imbalances exacerbated by globalization and to ensure broad-reaching reforms aimed at promoting growth and reducing the debt of individual states.
This is the commitment, and this resolution underscores it for all countries.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, I have cast a favorable vote on the report by my colleague Mattera, which examines the situation of single mothers, who today, as already stated, represent more than 5% of the population of the European Union.
The European Union therefore has a moral obligation to protect them, and the text approved today supports policies that can provide concrete assistance to these women, such as facilitation for women.
Healthcare, flexible work opportunities enabling the reconciliation of private and professional life, immediate access to affordable prices, and special unemployment benefits.
Only by adopting these measures can the needs of a group that is becoming increasingly significant in all advanced and industrialized countries be met.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the budget sets a few clear and important objectives within the framework of the EU 2020 strategy; for this reason, thank you to the rapporteurs.
It is essential to focus on investments, and not merely on the prudent maintenance of the budget.
The alternative would be to passively witness the speculative philosophy that characterizes our age.
We can no longer afford to follow individual interests; we must finally represent a single, cohesive subject, ready for confrontation.

I congratulate the commitments made regarding pilot projects and preparatory actions under heading 2, while ensuring that funding for the integrated maritime policy does not come at the expense of other initiatives and programmes in the fisheries sector.

This will enable us to implement new policies for improved management of marine resources through thorough research and data exchange regarding fish stocks, establishing new initiatives that in the Adriatic region may serve as best practices for other marine areas.

I conclude with a brief note for future discussions concerning the ECB, and in particular regarding the fishing fund: I hope greater effort will be made in controlling fishing, the core of the fight against illegal fishing, but also the foundation for rebuilding fish stocks.
that fish be considered common property.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the economic, financial, and social crisis we are currently experiencing has clearly highlighted several weaknesses in the European economic and social model.

I believe one of the most alarming and representative indicators of the current situation is the nearly six million jobs lost over the past three years—figures that are likely to increase unless a prompt and effective response is implemented.

The text by colleague Bastos emphasizes that, in order to make concrete progress, a stronger synergy between education, training, and the labor market is required, along with policies aimed at ensuring a greater alignment between the demands and supply within this market.
For these reasons, Mr. President, I have cast my vote in favor.
I voted in favor of what was presented by Member Angelilli because it addresses an issue that deeply concerns me as a mother and as a grandmother.
Protecting children from sexual exploitation, abuse, and the abhorrent world of pedopornography means defending our own children, our grandchildren, and consequently, the most vulnerable.
greater prevention, tougher criminal sanctions against those who commit such offenses, and more careful protection of children on these key points of the document adopted today.
Without doubt, the path is difficult.

I am particularly thinking of the dissemination of child pornography over the internet, which can only be combated through close cooperation among member states, police authorities, and service providers.
The challenges posed by modernity and the colleague Senata Fidelis's report, to which we have expressed our favorable vote, compel us to place the citizen in their role as consumer beyond any debate.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the phosphates present in household laundry detergents end up in water systems, flowing into European rivers, streams, and lakes.

Phosphorus triggers an acceleration in the reproduction of various forms of life; consequently, algae and other rapidly developing plant species proliferate excessively when phosphorus levels increase in an aquatic environment.
This excessive algal growth deprives other living organisms of oxygen and can also produce toxins that destroy fish and plant species, thus reducing the water's quality itself.

I therefore expressed my favourable vote on the text adopted today, as it is desirable to reduce the amount of phosphorus discharged into the waters of our continent in order to purify Europe's water resources.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the European Union has produced an enormous amount of legislation and regulations to build European law and has created the European area, known as the Stockholm Programme.
There is a mismatch between this body of legislation, this legislative monument, and the extent to which citizens actually use it, which is numerically and proportionally very low.
On the other hand, national judges and the national lawyers of the individual states have grown up within the law of their respective countries and have predominantly received training in this field.
This is the meaning: while we believe that the true European judge is the national judge who transforms his mindset and becomes imbued with an understanding of European law.

For this reason, before discussing training, it is essential to engage in extensive communication with citizens and legal professionals, so that they become advocates of this major innovation and help foster the willingness to take advantage of the benefits offered by this right.

But above all, it must be a bottom-up movement within in-service training; one cannot conduct lessons as if addressing schoolchildren in the judicial training of adult individuals who are professionally highly accomplished.

One must stimulate curiosity when handling one's own cases, connect with other colleagues, and seek solutions, as today's common law and civil law systems are converging and are no longer as distant from each other as they once were within the legal culture.

This is the core issue in our proposals, and I am pleased that the European Commission has come to recognize these aspects. In our view, we are calling for a reversal of trend—more legal studies rather than judicial training.
There is an example in the Netherlands called Eurinfra, very interesting; it is a network of officials in each court who disseminate this knowledge and encourage judges in this regard.
Here, the essence of our project is this; we hope it will receive the Commission's full support.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, I would like to begin by expressing my sincere appreciation for the work carried out by my colleague Giegold in this own-initiative report, and I also wish to recall that with him, and with others, we share an active presence in the Parliament’s Social Economy Intergroup.

My political group, as you know, considers the social model to be one of the foundational elements of the European integration project, not merely one policy among many, but a policy that defines our very project.

And in these times, with attacks linked to necessities, public spending cuts, and so on, we still firmly defend this conviction, this priority. We know it can be an updated, modified social model, but we also believe it must be maintained, defended, and reaffirmed precisely as a priority.

Therefore, in this same vision, we must also recognize and value economic subjects engaged in enterprise and the production of goods and services, such as cooperatives, which play a very specific role in the so-called European social economy.

They emphasize that production can be carried out, good outcomes can be achieved, and efficiency can be attained even without profit as the objective, for instance when the goals are employment, welfare, the valorization of work, and contribution to the life of the local community.

That is why, Mr. President, I conclude that it is essential for Europe to provide the necessary tools for an active policy, and to establish a good statute—not the one currently in place, which is unworkable—but a good statute for cooperatives could be a way of supporting this so important sector.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the role of the cooperative movement has always been crucial in building a more balanced and fair economic dimension.
It is a time of great turning point, and at this moment, as we are experiencing one of the most severe economic and social crises since the war, the role of cooperative societies becomes crucial.

if there has been a strong development of the cooperative dimension in the economy, there has consequently been an increase in employment, more skilled work, and a more balanced redistribution of produced wealth.
It is therefore positive that the European Union has adopted a statute for cooperative societies, but now more than ever it is necessary that there be a genuine simplification of the rules.

We need to give new momentum to the establishment of a common European framework in the field of cooperation, and this must be achieved by providing support rather than creating bureaucratic obstacles.
For this reason, I voted in favor of this resolution and I hope the Commission and the Council will actively work to address the issues mentioned.

The salvation of millions of unemployed Europeans does not depend on the inadequate assistance provided by the European Regional Development Fund. The data collected reveal that most beneficiaries of the Fund fail to secure new employment, while the more fortunate are reabsorbed into the labor market through temporary contracts, sometimes as short as six months.
In many cases, the unemployed choose not to access this fund because they doubt its usefulness.
The FEG is not a remedy nor a band-aid for the disastrous consequences of a European trade policy dangerously and too lightly managed by the Commission.

To combat the scourge of unemployment, which is now structural, Europe must curb its excessive openness toward the markets of developing countries, whose actual competition is the primary cause of the collapse of our businesses.
As long as Europe does not cease promoting economic self-harm by maintaining a short-sighted stance toward global trade flows, our companies will continue to fail.
The fund can only provide temporary support, but it is not through training courses that our unemployed will find peace and security.

Perhaps, among the many benefits, this expansion beyond the EU helps us understand that the possibility of utilization, without genuine fear of costs, also supports democracy.
In Europe, there are over 120 million broadband users, and at least 70 million citizens who use their mobile devices to connect to the internet for work, study, or simply to stay informed.
Thanks to this regulation, the European Union has finally decided to act in favor of all these consumers by reducing the cost of international voice, data, and SMS traffic.
A positive decision, as the price reduction program will result in an average decrease of tariffs by 35% over the next three years.

However, this is not enough in a difficult moment such as the current one, especially from an economic and employment standpoint; communication, new technologies, and innovation are a primary asset that Europe must defend with greater courage.
Every European citizen must be able to travel freely between Member States, benefiting from the telephone tariff set by their national operator.

End frauds and exorbitant bills for those traveling within the EU. If we truly wish to provide citizens with a concrete response, we should therefore establish a Schengen area for telecommunications, eliminating different tariffs for citizens depending on the country they are in.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, I was part of the European Parliament delegation that visited Sicily in November of last year.
Our delegation head, Cecilia Wistrom, wrote a letter to the Italian minister, the Interior Minister and Chancellor, and received a response that provided us with fairly clear answers.

I wish to emphasize above all the fact that following that visit to Italy, the government changed, and so did the approach to the situation, but the situation itself also changed in the countries from which the migratory flow predominantly originated.
However, this must not prevent us from undertaking a careful analysis of what has occurred and also of the current situation.
The most significant issue that remains relevant today is Lampedusa's status as an unsafe port, along with the closure of the first-aid and reception center in Contrada Imbriagola following the fire

in April of this year, after witnessing 10 people die on the overcrowded boat they were traveling on, or the 20 who were transferred from Linosa due to lack of space in other reception centers, or the 18 who still live in the stadium changing rooms on Linosa.

The tourist residence in Cala Creta, where they are currently housed, is certainly not a CIE nor a "cara," and above all, in the meantime both the healthcare system and the possibility of applying for asylum have been dismantled on Lampedusa.
Everything depends on the goodwill and kindness of the duty-bound carabinieri or of the citizens and volunteers of Lampedusa.
Another consideration to make is the role that the center itself had assumed in the recent past.

Allowing thousands upon thousands of people to gather in a location that, due to its capacity and intended function, could accommodate only a few hundred, in direct violation of every legal and ethical standard.

Allowing a territory of just a few square kilometers—already difficult due to its location and logistical challenges—to be forced to accommodate a number of people exceeding its own population must have had further consequences.
We must reflect on the responsibilities regarding a situation of extreme tension.
It is not possible that he was in arrest during the fire itself.
I believe the excellent report by colleague Rinaldi was successful in the Chamber.
I fully share the favorable assessment of the report.
Nevertheless, I would like to raise a minor concern: our Europe, in relation to this new world striving to find democracy and different economic conditions, is a Europe divided by the Mediterranean.

It is precisely a particular attention to the Mediterranean that I would like the Commission to focus on, both in terms of environmental aspects and in terms of the economic transactions taking place in the Mediterranean.
Nedran is a fishing location.
We are establishing a new fishing policy in Europe, which will set very strict rules on Europe's part.
On the other hand, however, there are no rules.
And then why not use fishing agreements and provide support to these countries so that they implement the same rules that apply in Europe?

This would be beneficial for European fishing, beneficial for the sea, for environmental conservation, and for ensuring that this rift becomes increasingly less divided, and instead more unified—a homogeneous space upon which to build Europe's future and that of this new world.

With the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon, the European Parliament has become a full-fledged institutional subject in the field of security policies and therefore has the right to actively and decisively participate in shaping the priorities of the internal security strategy and the Union's model of security.

The rapporteur Borsellino's report identifies the five key areas for which concrete actions have been proposed and calls for greater judicial and police cooperation among Member States to combat organized crime and terrorism.

I consider it particularly important to give strong consideration to the development of prevention policies, which are fundamental tools for ensuring a European space of freedom, security, and justice.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, with the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon, the foundations have been laid for the development of an integrated criminal law within Community law.

Emphasizing the importance of mutual recognition, the harmonization of protection standards, and the principle of presumed innocence, it is essential to underscore that the law must be extremely clear and easily understandable, so that it is evident to all what actions constitute or do not constitute a crime.
This holds particularly true for directives that must be mutually compatible with national law.
Any lack of charity could, to some extent, lead to divergent interpretations of the law.

I therefore believe that only a close collaboration among European institutions regarding the principles and methods to be followed can ensure a coherent and high-quality European strategy in the field of criminal law.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the European Union safeguards the general interests of consumers against unfair business practices, misleading advertising, and abusive contractual terms, but there is no solution.
It is questionable whether consumers who are more vulnerable due to age, physical or mental health issues, or simply a lack of adequate information should be afforded more careful protection.
I therefore expressed my favourable vote on the text submitted by my colleague Perez, as I agree that the development of a strategy for consumer law
of the so-called most vulnerable contributes not only to their social inclusion, but also to ensuring a safer, fairer, and more competitive single market.
The colleague Perez's report, which we have expressed our favorable vote on, addresses the need to consider drafting a report for the future.

A strategy is aimed at protecting the rights of the most vulnerable consumers, in preparation for the strategy that the European Commission and Member States will be called upon to develop in this regard.
The conditions of consumer vulnerability arise from causes of different natures and potentially affect everyone.

For this reason, it is necessary to provide protection for this category alongside the general protection of the consumer, and to develop a targeted strategy in this regard, which, by taking into account the composite nature of the issue, avoids

On one hand, the formulation of static and rigid definitions incapable of accounting for all the nuances of the issue and the various possible forms of vulnerability; on the other, the prevention of the creation of new forms of discrimination.
It is therefore necessary to establish specific and targeted protective measures.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the management of bluefin tuna fishing has a long and rather complex history dating back to the 1990s and, to date, is probably the most strictly regulated by the European Union.
Despite substantial investments in monitoring, which have led to greater compliance with regulations, uncertainties regarding the issue of overfishing still remain.
I have therefore expressed my support for the Romeva i Rueda report, which proposes a reduction in the allowable catch rate, greater control, and strengthened measures to reduce capacity.
Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the relations between the European Union and China clearly reveal situations of imbalance at the commercial level.
It is therefore incumbent upon European institutions to strive to assist European enterprises in overcoming the crisis and to make access to international markets more equitable.
I therefore expressed my support for this report, particularly endorsing the rapporteur's emphasis on applying the principle of reciprocity to relations.
the relations between the European Union and China to ensure fair competition on international markets and the demand for greater protection of intellectual property rights.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the road ahead is still long, but today's step may prove to be an important one toward the eventual introduction and implementation of a tax on financial transactions.
I understand the prudence, caution, and need for gradualism expressed by Commissioner Schmetta, as well as the technical issues rightly highlighted by Minister Vestager.

But precisely for this reason, I hope the Parliament will express a clear political will, with a broad majority, to provide momentum and impetus to the necessary initiatives aimed at addressing the issues that remain unresolved, particularly at the level of the Council.
We all want to carefully assess the impact of this tax, but it is unfair to raise unwarranted fears, which may lead to contradictory and counterproductive decisions.
I am referring, for example, to the logic underlying the proposal of exemptions.
If one is attentive to reducing the tax's impact, then it is important to have the broadest possible tax base, with a low rate, rather than seeking to extend exemptions indefinitely.
To those concerned about the impact on small investors, I recall the progressive nature of the tax, which imposes a greater burden on those who carry out more transactions and make more investments.
of transactions, and it is clear that competent authorities will have to monitor, in any case, that no attempt is made to pass on any costs to customers and end users.
I therefore hope that the Parliament will deliver a vote by a wide majority in favor of this tax.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, since 1990, through the instrument of macrofinancial assistance, the European Union has provided economic aid to countries experiencing short-term balance of payments difficulties in the form of grants, loans, or a combination of both.
The rather lengthy timelines, combined with the difficulties highlighted by the decision-making criteria used to date, have made it necessary to formulate some amendments to the regulation.

I therefore welcome the proposal introduced in the new regulation, which aims to make evaluation criteria faster and more effective, and to establish clearer rules and conditions for the disbursement of macro-financial assistance.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, approximately 100 million European citizens are engaged in various forms of voluntary activity.

From major sporting events to cultural gatherings, volunteers play a crucial role, and we must not overlook the work of those who rush to provide assistance and solidarity in the event of major natural or environmental disasters.
Volunteering represents one of the broadest expressions of active citizenship and a crucial activity for the development of democratic values and equality within the European Union.
For this reason, I advocate the development of high-quality national and cross-border volunteering and the elimination of existing barriers.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, from communications to finance, transportation, energy, culture, and healthcare, the role played by technology is increasingly significant.
The increasingly frequent use of the Internet and digital tools makes the issue of cybersecurity one of the European agenda's top priorities.

Information and communication technologies can indeed promote economic and social development only if existing regulations ensure user security with regard to data privacy and intellectual property rights.

For this reason, only a comprehensive international cooperation involving member states, institutions, and industry stakeholders can establish a robust system capable of implementing effective measures to ensure cybersecurity.
Madam President, thanks to the Commissioner, you have said important and shareable things, which are very significant.
I find it truly, as he said, unsettling that we have each lived in our respective countries with the figures and data we previously heard from colleague Oconio.
2020. Yet it is equally clear that this scandal has truly highlighted the need for a careful assessment of the current European legislation on medical devices.

Unfortunately, this fraud has highlighted the shortcomings we have discussed so far in this Chamber, which the Commissioner has so ably represented; therefore, I would not wish to dwell on this aspect within the limited time available to me.
I believe it is appropriate to mobilize everything necessary so that we can offer the best possible to our citizens.
But I believe this scandal, which has taught us so much, could also serve as an opportunity to take a further step forward, and I echo what my colleague just said.

The communication directed toward patients is truly poor; only the positive aspects are continually emphasized, with claims of a kind of eternal youth and promised beauty, yet no prostheses are entirely risk-free.

I believe this awareness, especially when discussing purely aesthetic surgery, must be clearly communicated to people, to young women, since we know there are very young individuals who are approaching this type of procedure.
So I see that you are genuinely absent, and therefore I strongly urge you: let's really work, the time is tight, we should have already done it yesterday.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the Council's regulation concerning the multiannual programme for the stock exchange has been adopted prior to the entry into force of the Treaty, and therefore alignment has become necessary.

This regulation, the first in the fisheries sector to be aligned with the Treaty, therefore holds considerable importance, as the solution adopted in this case could affect the decision-making procedure applicable to other multiannual plans.
For this reason, I express my vote in favor.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, from Seveso to Bhopal to Toulouse, there have been numerous environmental disasters in recent years that have affected Europe and jeopardized the health of European citizens.

The text by colleague Ader addresses the revision of Directive SES II, which aims to limit the effects of incidents involving significant quantities of hazardous and highly polluting substances released into the environment.
The revised standards, which will take effect starting in June 2015, aim to protect the environment and, above all, to prevent tragic health consequences for citizens; therefore, I voted in favor.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, I too thank Stevenson for the excellent work and the approach he has brought to this report, which substantially addresses the two main issues.

Here it is not merely a matter of a herring management plan; fundamentally, it is about securing the future for fishermen in certain respects and implementing maritime planning to ensure, over the long term, that this sector is adequately protected.
But the key issue of this report remains the matter of delegated acts.

I believe the Council should accept the approach set out in the Treaty of Lisbon, and I also think it is now time for the Parliament, in some way, to speak with a voice perhaps more authoritative than that of the European Parliament.
of a debate within the Fisheries Committee or of a debate on a report concerning trawling, must have the courage to take a very clear and firm stance on the issue of delegated acts.
We claim the right that, in these matters, the ordinary procedure is genuinely applied.
So perfectly that the issue involves, in addition to many other fishing sectors—some of which will worsen compared to the ongoing reform—also matters relating to agriculture and other topics.
The essential step is respecting the will of Parliament.
In preparing this report, no consideration whatever was given to the interested parties, the transport companies.
In Italy, 91% of freight transport takes place by road.
Their rights will be disregarded if this report is approved in its current form.
In many committees of this Parliament, we daily strive to reduce the financial and administrative burden weighing on our businesses, but reports like this undermine such efforts.
We must make every effort, as they will suffocate the companies themselves with new bureaucratic burdens, devastating economic losses, and new forms of unfair competition.

It would have taken very little to achieve a balanced report that did not overlook the right to survival of our businesses during a period of great economic difficulty, but such a dialogue was not possible.

On the contrary, the report deliberately ignored even previous legislative acts of the European Union, such as the 2002 Working Time Directive, and even overlooked Commission documents acknowledging the dangers of practices such as retrofitting, which are instead revived in this report.
The document we will vote on tomorrow, colleagues, will ask you to forget the positive achievements made in recent years, and once again, it will be European companies that will bear the costs.

Just to say, Mr. President, that ACTA was a most serious political mistake—setting aside the legal aspect—it triggered a reaction across the world of immense proportions, and that alone is sufficient to prove it was a political error.
But why the Bense?
Because it created the suspicion that access to the web was truly being closed off, which would be a major modern revolution.
And why is that?
Because he made the mistake of combining counterfeiting and piracy, which are two clearly distinct phenomena.
The way of combating counterfeiting has nothing to do with copyright.
Then there is another error, one that has excluded Parliament from an important activity it has been carrying out for some time—namely, the creation of a new copyright, a new right of authorship.
The old copyright law that existed before the web. The point is, we must work on copyright law and set aside ACTA, which is an obstacle, reject it, and exclude it, because it hinders our work.
Fortunately, after months and years of hesitation, the Commission has brought two significant measures—on orphan works and the upcoming one on copyright management societies—before Parliament.
Then, let's work on this by reflecting with our minds, not just emotions, but without being hindered by preconceptions.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, I simply wish to add my voice to the acclaim this report has received in this House regarding the need to strengthen the European Union's capacity to address the challenges of the future.
of being a place where it is possible to invest, produce, and produce with the aim of our vision of an inclusive economy, socially and environmentally sustainable.

Therefore, I believe some of these guidelines here are very useful within this provision, others can be added, and I think it is important to gather this indication, working increasingly to make attractiveness the primary focus.
consequence of territorial policies, territorial marketing, and openness, whereby the territory itself proposes a productive, industrial vocation.

that arises from the territory and is respectful of all environmental and simultaneously European political compatibilities moving in this direction, a voice emerging from the territory and from Europe that attracts investment and fosters growth.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, when discussing sensitive issues such as the authorization of products and substances for use in organic production, we are talking about safeguarding the health of European citizens.

It is indeed important to meet consumer expectations regarding the quality of organic products circulating in European markets, and it is equally important to ensure adequate implementation of Community legislation by relevant authorities, bodies, and operators.
For this reason, I express my favorable vote on the colleague's text.
Pia of the initial comments on the Council, the phase of reductionist reading has now begun, and this is wrong, as significant progress has been made.
Consider, for example, the breakdown of destructive ties between banks and public debts, and the admittedly weak plan for employment and work.

It seems that the spirit of revitalization and a renewed drive toward political unity is once again emerging within European institutions, after so much sluggishness and inertia, and a determination to achieve tangible progress toward deeper integration.
We have recorded a strong alignment between the Parliament and the Commission on this.

I am not speaking of winners and losers, but it is undeniable that there has been a new institutional and political vitality compared to the previous diarchy, which was the negation, indeed the very antithesis, of the communal spirit.
There has been an initiative and activism by multiple countries, not to create new exclusive alliances, but to build a shared consensus respectful of all member states.
This is the path toward the political objective of unity, without which any banking, fiscal, and monetary union will be entirely inadequate.
June 6, with the Council's decision to restore to Member States the possibility of reintroducing border controls, a new Europe was born—one courageous in fulfilling the desire of all.
and greater security for its citizens, is finally inspired by a more mature interpretation of the concept of solidarity, brought back to the defense of rights above all for its peoples.

To President Schulz, even though he is not present, I say that this historic decision does not represent an interinstitutional incident, but rather a respect for the will of the citizens, and not for the eurobureaucracy that for years has pretended not to understand what is truly happening beyond the glass palace.
No imaginary influx of irregular migrants, Mr. President Schulz, but an increase in illegal immigrants, measured by Frontex, of 35 percent in 2011.

I regret only that the political skirmishes enacted in this Chamber and during the previous plenary session have taken time away from a more useful and constructive activity—reflecting on why it has been the Member States, rather than us, who have defended the interests of our citizens.

If the Parliament fails to find its own soul and the courage to oppose complacency, and continues to drift on the sterile platitudes on which it has been treading for too long, it will remain a follower rather than a leader in building the European project.
I would like to draw the attention of this House to the fact that yesterday a
We have officially announced that the Higgs boson has been measured and concretely identified.
the particle that gives mass to all others and their existence, and this occurred in European soil.
Science gives life to our land, civilization, and well-being, and for a moment we may aspire to once again become a world capital of science.
Utopia is the trait of the human being who invents a utopia and wishes to realize it, and it is for science and for this wonderful ambition of humanity that today a person
A blind person who has no eyes can see with their hands or read a book with their ears, and a young South African boy who has no legs can run in the Olympics in a sprint competition.
Well then, Europe must be the homeland of science; this great event, which will open up new frontiers of knowledge, must be fully supported by Europe.
Mr. President, honourable colleagues, victims of crime within the European Union too often refrain from filing complaints due to fear or lack of trust in institutions.
The text adopted today aims to strengthen the rights of victims of crime and ensure them the same level of protection, assistance, and access to justice under safe conditions.
all Member States, with particular attention to those victims who face a high risk of suffering further harm or intimidation during the criminal proceedings.

Of particular importance, therefore, is the recognition of victim status not only for individuals who have suffered harm as a result of a crime, but also for family members and the deceased person following the crime.
On September 11, the Catalan people peacefully demonstrated in Barcelona.
One million people have requested independence for Catalonia from Spain.
On October 15th, the British Prime Minister Cameron signed the agreement to allow a referendum on Scotland's independence from London.
In Veneto, my region, in recent weeks thousands of people have signed in order to hold a referendum on independence from Veneto and Italy.
Yesterday, in Veneto, Lombardy, and all the regions of the North, hundreds and thousands of people signed to gain greater autonomy from Rome.
Yet even in Flanders and the Basque Country, citizens are loudly demanding autonomy and independence from national states.
What does President Schulz say?
It calls for a courageous commitment to clearly say no to all separatist tendencies.
His deputy, Vidal Quadra, even worse, has explicitly called for the use of force against Catalan citizens.
Their statements constitute a very serious act, contrary to the principles of the democracy that here we preach and that we must defend.
I tell them that it will be the peoples who will write the future of the new Europe, not the bureaucrats in Brussels.
The path is already marked.
The austerity policy adopted to overcome the economic crisis has not yielded the expected results.
As highlighted in the rapporteur La Via's report, only by investing in a more selective manner and supporting the ideal of a European budget that is sometimes overly constrained.
To support growth and employment, it will be possible to rebalance the economies of the Member States, thereby improving the situation of European citizens.
For this reason, I therefore express my vote in favor.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the social and economic context in which the multiannual financial framework for the period 2014 is being discussed is rather problematic.

With member states committed to implementing fiscal adjustments within their respective national budgets to address public finances, the European Union is viewed in some cases as an entity that exacerbates the financial burden on taxpayers.

For this reason, to truly overcome the crisis and secure a better future for young Europeans, it will be of fundamental importance to support increased and more concrete investments in the fields of education and development.
This is the strategic plan of an institution, from which one understands what it is, what it does, and above all, what it aims to achieve in the future.

We face a crucial choice between a survival budget, a dragging-on budget, perhaps a little less burdensome for everyone, or a budget of courage, of selective choices, of investments for employment, innovation, and young people—that is, for the future of the European Union and its vocation for development.

Everyone should understand that, as colleague Calfin clearly stated among others, Europe today can accomplish what individual states can no longer achieve for the economy, regional development, and territories where social cohesion either strengthens or undermines the lives of citizens we have discussed.
Today, Europe's major decisions risk being stifled by accounting disputes within the Council, not only among beneficiary countries, net contributors, and recipient countries.

We argue that this is a false problem, because it is easy to demonstrate with figures and numbers—not with words—what has occurred in the past, that contributing countries, sometimes more than others, benefit from economic convergence and growth.
The Council is currently working to assign figures to the various headings.

The war of positions has begun, with statements and positions taken by some states that regard the budget as a source of expenditure that can be reduced without hesitation, indeed, perhaps even useful in reinforcing an easy-to-activate anti-European sentiment during times of economic crisis.
It is a mistaken position that does not help the integration we are striving to achieve in other areas.
Dear colleagues, our Europe remains incomplete and maimed, as we have often stated.
We have adopted a stability pact, which was supposed to also promote development, but we have stabilized so much that we are now in recession.
Here, let us at least seize the opportunity offered by this budget, this multiannual framework, to make choices in the right direction.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the lack of information and awareness regarding one's rights, compounded by economic barriers, is not a solution.
Language barriers and the non-recognition of diplomas and qualifications are the most common problems encountered by EU citizens when residing, studying, or working in another EU country.
Unfortunately, there remains a lack of visibility regarding the concept of Union citizenship and its tangible benefits.
For this reason, I have expressed my support for 2013 as the European Year of the Citizens.

The primary objective is therefore to strengthen awareness of the rights associated with EU citizenship and to assist citizens in fully exercising their right to move freely and reside freely within the territories of the Member States.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the growing number of innovative financial instruments poses numerous challenges in terms of regulation, governance, and monitoring their effectiveness.
A proper balance must indeed be struck between the objectives of transparency and oversight, as well as a sufficient level of effectiveness and speed of implementation.

The introduction of such instruments at the European level remains highly significant, as it provides a means through which the European Union, particularly during times of crisis, can stimulate investment in the economy.
real, in accordance with its own objectives, in a context where the continuous decline in contributions destined for the EU budget contrasts with the steady expansion of its political ambitions.
The Brussels I Regulation has undoubtedly represented one of the most effective pieces of European legislation for safety and health at work.
of Union legislation, as it has laid the foundations for a European judicial area promoted and ensured through legal certainty among European citizens.
The dissemination of this regulation touches upon fundamental issues for the development of European justice, including the extension of rules on
on the jurisdiction over disputes involving parties domiciled in third countries and the agreements concerning the choice of competent forum.
I am convinced that the adoption of the new regulation can ensure stronger protection for consumers and greater legal certainty within the internal market, and for this reason I have voted in favor.

Mr. President, Commissioner, today Europe is being called upon to undertake a new task: coordinating industrial policies, and even the most stringent advocates of autonomy and private initiative are now demanding the exercise of this role.
Without a greater European integration of national strategies, even in terms of employment protection, the current crisis will not be overcome and there will be no revival.
The crisis in the automotive industry is not merely one of overproduction capacity; it is a dramatic loss of employment.
But the crisis now appears to be structural, affecting the very destiny of the car, of the product, as it exists today.
We want to keep the automobile industry in Europe and we want to avoid dispersing existing expertise and know-how, as well as professional skills.
We therefore call for the initiation of a major restructuring and revitalization project, and we urge that it be carried out as soon as possible, as costs are proportional to time.
Restructuring cannot be merely the result of fragmented national decisions or individual firms.
Efforts must at least be made to coordinate and steer this painful process at the community level, bringing the relevant stakeholders to the table through social dialogue.
producers, trade unions, and individual national governments, offering the support of research, innovation, human capital training, requalification, and the necessary funding.
I know it will be a difficult task, as the national interests of individual companies are not aligned.

But since the current competitive struggle within the European Union is highly ambiguous and sometimes risks being unfair, I believe it is ultimately in the interest of all countries to have effective community-driven impetus and leadership to jointly face the increasingly aggressive global market.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the energy crisis has imposed and continues to impose an extremely high cost on employment, affecting thousands of workers—both precarious and stable, as well as temporary workers—who are numerous in this sector.
The major steel conglomerates are increasingly turning toward the Far East or the American continent, posing a significant question regarding the direction of our development model.
I believe Europe must regain the understanding that a manufacturing system such as ours requires robust basic production capabilities able to supply the European internal market.
We must therefore defend this sector, the role of a stable steel industry, and we will support it, Mr. Commissioner, if we wish to halt industrial decline.

I believe we must move beyond a dualism that has not been reached here this evening, but which often arises in discussions on this subject—a dualism between environmental advocates and workers' interests—that is mistaken because it prevents us from envisioning a future perspective.
An industry that views steelmaking as an integral part must rely on advanced production systems and concrete, balanced solutions that safeguard diverse interests and differing needs.

Therefore, we need strong, even European-level, planning focused on sustainability, which, in my view, we must not retreat from, as it represents a genuine added value with which we wish to compete globally, supporting investment.

We have repeatedly emphasized new technologies, new processes, the modernization of facilities for a European economy that makes effective use of resources, including energy resources—indeed, starting with energy resources—and also the search for new and different materials.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, as we have recalled in this Chamber, next December the United Nations agency dedicated to information and communication technologies will convene in Dubai for the World Conference on International Communications.
The subject is the renegotiation of international regulations on telecommunications.

We cannot forget; I am pleased with the words of Commissioner Tajani, the work accomplished thus far, which cannot be called into question, nor can we allow the authority of the European Union to be circumvented.

a supranational organization such as the ITU, of which we know hundreds of states from around the world are members, many of which are known for issues related to democracy, transparency, and freedom of expression.
Therefore, it is not incorrect to consider the pillars of the Internet—namely freedom, participation, openness, and innovation—to be at risk.

Therefore, I believe it is necessary to safeguard the role of the Internet and the potential that the Internet, in particular, can have in revitalizing economic growth, not only that but also in ensuring opportunities for all.
I therefore hope that the Commission works towards ensuring that Europe at Dubai is united and speaks with one voice.

Many are the controversial proposals presented at the negotiating table of the World Conference on International Telecommunications, particularly those suggesting changes to Internet governance, which challenge network neutrality and could have negative implications for consumers.
Therefore, Internet freedom—or more precisely, freedom of expression and data protection—is at risk.
Indeed, what some states have proposed would allow a government not only to intrude into the communications of its own citizens, but even into the data flowing across its own networks.
The step from here to the limitation of freedom of expression and censorship is extremely short, and therefore I believe it contradicts the fundamental principles of the European Union.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, I have expressed my support for the colleague's text, and in particular, I agree with the rapporteur in emphasizing the fundamental role played by national judges in applying the law within the Union.

I therefore consider it necessary to fully support the European Union's efforts aimed at improving and coordinating training on jurisdiction for judicial and administrative authorities and legal professionals.
Moreover, all initiatives in the sector should be intensified to fully realize the concept of a Union based on the rule of law.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, in recent years, the extraction of so-called unconventional hydrocarbons, including shale gas and oil, has brought about an unprecedented transformation in global energy markets.
Europe, in order to address this situation and become less dependent on hydrocarbon imports, will therefore have to rely on its own potential gas and oil reserves.
In this regard, some Member States have already authorized shale gas exploration and are preparing for the extraction phase, should discovery make it possible.
In such a context, it becomes necessary to monitor the regulations concerning shale gas extraction within the Union's borders, so that the effects of its extinction are not reduced.
The extractions, undoubtedly economically viable, do not create environmental problems due to the potential release of harmful substances into groundwater or the atmosphere.
Mr. President, honourable colleagues, I believe today should be the occasion to speak objectively about Kazakhstan and the important agreement the country is negotiating with the European Union.
Kazakhstan is a crucial country in terms of the stability and strategic future of Central Asia and is undoubtedly an important economic and commercial partner of the European Union.
It is a country that has already initiated a process of economic reform and also a democratic process, but still needs to make considerable efforts to fully complete these processes.

It is, however, a country that with this objective continues to demonstrate its readiness for new and more solid reforms, and I believe this is precisely the task that the European Union should undertake in supporting them.

For this reason, I am confident that the signing of this enhanced agreement will enable progress, as its approval will strengthen an already solid and productive relationship and ensure sustained support from the European Union.

I remain convinced—and I believe this should have been emphasized even more in the report we will vote on tomorrow—that progress toward democracy and human rights must be accompanied by a renewed, open, and constructive mutual commitment.

Our objective must not be to adopt a negative stance toward these and other countries due to the lack of immediate implementation of changes; our objective must instead be to support these countries' efforts to achieve the goal, including the respect for the principles of human rights.

Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, esteemed colleagues, I would like to thank the rapporteurs of the reports we will vote on tomorrow, and I would merely make a brief remark on the issue of spin-offs. I fully align myself with the unanimous sentiment expressed by all colleagues this evening.
However, I would like to focus on the Ferrera report, which I greet and commend.
I meant to say that, with the previous common fishing policy, we have only contributed to increasing the gap between the institutions governing fishing and its true actors, namely the fishermen.

Unfortunately, up to now in Europe, the lack of a genuine definition of artisanal fishing has disadvantaged those who have always regarded fishing as an activity tied to the traditions of their land, seas, and cuisine.

Thus grew the conviction that the path pursued by the European Commission aimed to standardize all fishing in Europe under large-scale industrial fishing, guided solely by market logic and disregarding the concerns of thousands of fishermen who, for a couple of

For decades, they have operated according to rules related to the preservation of the ecosystem, which I can only support, but which have never safeguarded the livelihoods of the microeconomy of artisanal fishing.
This own-initiative report is a strong signal to the Commission and could translate into concrete actions benefiting the fishing microeconomy.
The report, in fact, highlights a number of advantages arising from allocating a minimal amount to small-scale fishing through the European Fund.
Projects with integrated solutions that benefit the coastal community as a whole must be encouraged, and, Commissioner, it will be essential to promote young people's access to job opportunities.

In this sector, we have discussed this matter several times in committee, because otherwise not only will fishing likely die out due to the depletion of marine areas and fish stocks, but it will also likely disappear because young people are no longer drawn to this field.
This report provides an opportunity to include training and motivational initiatives for young people who wish to enter the sector.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, like many of my fellow Members, today I ask the Council, which has put forward this proposal: what do you intend to do, gentlemen of Europe?
Do you want to reduce it to a minimum, freezing it in the future?
But it is the same Europe that subsequently seeks to relaunch through the Van Rompuy document on integration.
But with what consistency, I ask you, does Varro Poirier handle these two files?

And what consistency can citizens rediscover between the decisions of July, employment and growth, the loud appeals to hope and the future within the European Union, which we also hear from the Council, and this sudden, violent collapse, in the name of realism, into a perspective of bare survival?
How will it be possible, with these figures and these cuts, to turn the much-praised major reforms of cohesion and the CAP into concrete actions and active policies?

Here, these are sincere questions; we would like a clear, honest response, because there are here signs of an institutional schizophrenia that Parliament no longer accepts, and that citizens no longer accept either.
The Parliament demands consistency, it demands credibility.

The first criticism is that at the budget level, we are far from achieving growth and investments—we are still stuck at the 2008 budget level. It must be acknowledged that, regarding heading 1a, Van Rompuy's proposal overturns the scandalous Cypriot assumption.
Indeed, here the role of the budget as an indispensable driver of growth in certain sectors, beyond national budgets, is acknowledged.

But let it be clear that neither growth nor innovation should come at the expense of other sectors that are equally part of growth and jobs, such as the cohesion policy and, I must add, dear colleagues, the agricultural policy, which has boosted employment and is increasingly oriented toward innovation and the protection of the territory.

Our territory, growth and jobs, culture, training, transnational networks, the CEF, as well as solidarity and international development, human rights worldwide, and our contribution to the Millennium Goals.

Before us, however, unfortunately, is the usual spectacle of the Council—no vision, no leadership, a babel of opposing and competing national interests, an inextricable hatred of vetoes, expectations, and widespread dissatisfaction.
And Europe, unfortunately, is the great absentee.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, shark finning is an environmentally unacceptable practice, as it threatens the sustainability of certain shark species, which are therefore at risk from this perspective.
social and economic, as it entails the waste of a food commodity with commercial value, as well as from an ethical standpoint, as it demonstrates a complete lack of respect for animal life.

Although this practice has been prohibited since 2003 in European waters and for the European fleet, the Commission has decided to submit a proposal aimed at eliminating the exemption allowing the removal of shark fins aboard Union fishing vessels.

This proposal, by further ensuring the protection that sharks require, will enable the European Union to actively contribute to the global effort underway to eradicate the cruel practice of shark finning in other parts of the world.
